questions.question:
A company is testing new features for its website. The new website version is installed on a different instance. The company wants to expose the new version to a handful of users to see if the features work as intended.

Which Amazon Route 53 routing policy can help meet the requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which Route 53 routing policy directs a small percentage of users to a new website version for testing?
choices: Weighted routing policy
Simple routing policy
Multivalue answer routing policy
Latency routing policy
correctAnswers: Weighted routing policy
questions.explanation: A weighted routing policy lets you associate multiple resources with a single domain name and control the proportion of traffic routed to each one by assigning relative weights. By giving the new website version a small weight, only a fraction of users are directed to it for testing while the rest continue to reach the existing version.

questions.question:
An online food delivery website is hosted in an Amazon ECS Cluster and is using an RDS database instance. The company has recently deployed the new version of its delivery website which uses Amazon Aurora Serverless instead of RDS. Your manager instructed you to disable the backups of your RDS instance as these will no longer be used. You were able to successfully set the retention period to 1 with no issues but you encountered an error when you tried to set this at 0.

Which of the following could be the possible reason for this issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Why can't you set the RDS backup retention period to 0?
choices: The minimum retention period that you can set is 1.
The RDS instance is using a Multi-AZ deployments configuration.
The RDS instance has Read Replicas.
You do not have the required IAM access to configure your RDS instance.
correctAnswers: The RDS instance has Read Replicas.
questions.explanation:
There are several reasons why you may need to set the backup retention period to 0. For example, you can disable automatic backups immediately by setting the retention period to 0. If you set the value to 0 and receive a message saying that the retention period must be between 1 and 35, check to make sure you haven’t setup a read replica for the instance. Read replicas require backups for managing read replica logs, thus, you can’t set the retention period of 0.

Hence, the correct answer: The RDS instance has Read Replicas.

The option that says: The RDS instance is using a Multi-AZ deployments configuration is incorrect because a Multi-AZ deployments configuration should have no effect on setting the backup retention period.

The option that says: The minimum retention period that you can set is 1 is incorrect because the minimum retention period that you can set is 0 and not 1.

The option that says: You do not have the required IAM access to configure your RDS instance is incorrect because although it can be a valid answer, you were able to set it to 1 without issues. This means that you already have the required IAM policy.

questions.question:
A business is serving thousands of media assets stored on Amazon S3 to customers all over the world. The company already has a CloudFront distribution in place with Amazon S3 as its origin. In order to improve content delivery to various devices, the company is deploying an on-the-fly image transformation. In line with this change, the CloudOps Engineer must think of a way to help reduce the number of requests hitting the origin and increase the percentage of content served directly by CloudFront.

Which action can help the CloudOps Engineer complete the task?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which CloudFront feature reduces origin requests and improves cache hit ratio for global content delivery?
choices: Enable the Origin Shield feature on the existing CloudFront distribution
Configure the existing CloudFront distribution to use signed cookies. Create an origin group and set up an origin failover to improve the cache hit ratio
Configure the existing CloudFront distribution to use the origin cache headers. Include the Cache-Control: max-age=0 header to the S3 objects.
Attach a caching policy with enabled compression support to the existing CloudFront distribution
correctAnswers: Enable the Origin Shield feature on the existing CloudFront distribution
questions.explanation:
CloudFront Origin Shield provides a centralized caching layer that sits in front of your origin to help increase CloudFront’s cache hit ratio and collapse simultaneous requests for the same object coming across multiple regions. As a general best practice, you should always choose the Origin Shield Region that is closest to your origin. When enabled, all origin fetches coming from any CloudFront Point of Presence or Regional Edge Cache will now be routed through the Origin Shield location for a final cache check. Any content not already held in the Origin Shield location will then benefit from central request consolidation so that as little as one request goes to the origin.

Origin Shield can be easily incorporated into any CloudFront workload. However, the most notable benefits are seen among workloads that have viewers spread across multiple regions, involve on-demand processes such as just-in-time packaging or on-the-fly image transformations, or on-premises origins with scaling or bandwidth constraints.

The requirement for the scenario is to improve the cache/hit ratio of the existing CloudFront distribution. This can be achieved by simply enabling the Origin Shield feature on the CloudFront distribution settings.

Therefore, the correct answer is: Enable the Origin Shield feature on the existing CloudFront distribution.

The option that says: Configure the existing CloudFront distribution to use the origin cache headers. Include the Cache-Control: max-age=0 header to the S3 objects is incorrect. The Cache-Control header specifies how long (in seconds) an object remains in the cache before CloudFront gets the object again from the origin server. Setting max-age to 0 instructs CloudFront to retrieve the object from the origin each time, which defeats the purpose of caching.

The option that says: Attach a caching policy with enabled compression support to the existing CloudFront distribution is incorrect. Compression only reduces object size, allowing objects to be downloaded faster. It won’t help improve the cache/hit ratio.

The option that says: Configure the existing CloudFront distribution to use signed cookies. Create an origin group and set up an origin failover to improve the cache hit ratio is incorrect. This has nothing to do with caching at all. Signed cookies allow you to control access to multiple restricted files without the need to change URLs. In addition, setting up an origin failover is primarily used for providing high availability and not for improving the cache hit ratio.

questions.question:
A company has an application deployed on Amazon EC2 instances that connects to a backend database. This application requires access to sensitive credentials, including the database username and password. During the CI/CD deployment process, AWS CodePipeline retrieves the application source code from a GitHub repository. The team aims to avoid the unintentional disclosure of sensitive information, particularly database credentials, and to ensure that these credentials are securely managed and made available to the application during deployment without hardcoding in the source code.

Which solution will meet these requirements? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which two AWS services securely store and provide database credentials to applications without hardcoding them?
choices: Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to store credentials. Retrieve these values securely during application startup and set them as environmental variables.
Use EC2 instance metadata to store the sensitive credentials and retrieve them during application startup.
Include the credentials in the source code managed by the GitHub repository and restrict repository access.
Store the credentials in the EC2 instance user data script and run the script during the instance launch.
Use AWS Secrets Manager to store secret strings securely. Configure the application to retrieve these secrets dynamically and set them as environmental variables during deployment.
correctAnswers: Use AWS Secrets Manager to store secret strings securely. Configure the application to retrieve these secrets dynamically and set them as environmental variables during deployment.
Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to store credentials. Retrieve these values securely during application startup and set them as environmental variables.
questions.explanation:
AWS Secrets Manager provides a secure method to store and retrieve sensitive information like database credentials. It supports automatic secrets rotation and fine-grained access control using AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM). Applications can securely retrieve secrets dynamically through API calls or environment variables during runtime, reducing security risks. Using AWS Secrets Manager meets the requirement to prevent hardcoding credentials in the source code and ensures secure access to sensitive information during deployment.

AWS Secrets Manager

AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store allows secure storage of configuration data and secrets with optional encryption using AWS Key Management Service (KMS). Applications can retrieve these parameters through API calls or during application startup, ensuring sensitive data is not exposed in source code or deployment artifacts. Systems Manager Parameter Store provides a secure way to store and access sensitive parameters, enabling secure handling of database credentials without embedding them in the source code.

AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Use AWS Secrets Manager to store secret strings securely. Configure the application to retrieve these secrets dynamically and set them as environmental variables during deployment.

– Use AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store to store credentials. Retrieve these values securely during application startup and set them as environmental variables.

The option that says: Include the credentials in the source code managed by the GitHub repository and restrict repository access is incorrect because embedding sensitive credentials in source code is a security risk. Even with restricted access, a repository compromise could expose the credentials.

The option that says: Store the credentials in the EC2 instance user data script and run the script during the instance launch is incorrect because EC2 instance metadata, including user data, can be accessed by anyone with appropriate permissions, typically leading to potential exposure of sensitive information.

The option that says: Use EC2 instance metadata to store the sensitive credentials and retrieve them during application startup is incorrect because instance metadata is not primarily designed to store sensitive data securely. Metadata can be accessed by any process running on the instance, which may lead to the unintended exposure of credentials.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer is overseeing a fleet of Windows-based Amazon EC2 instances within a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). The organization already has an established AWS Managed Microsoft Active Directory (AD) for user authentication. There’s a requirement for these EC2 instances to share a common file system using Amazon Elastic File System (Amazon EFS). After creating the EFS file share, the CloudOps Engineer encounters difficulties mounting it to the Windows EC2 instances.

What steps should the CloudOps Engineer follow to enable file sharing among the EC2 instances?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS file system service should replace EFS for shared storage on Windows EC2 instances with Active Directory?
choices: Remove the EFS file share. Establish a file share for the EC2 instances using Amazon FSx for Lustre.
Remove the EFS file share. Establish a file share for the EC2 instances using Amazon FSx for Windows File Server.
Set up the security groups and network ACL to allow NFS port (TCP port 2049).
Set up support for NFSv4.1 and install the NFS client to the Amazon EC2 instances.
correctAnswers: Remove the EFS file share. Establish a file share for the EC2 instances using Amazon FSx for Windows File Server.
questions.explanation:
Amazon EFS (Elastic File System) and Amazon FSx are two versatile file storage solutions offered by Amazon Web Services (AWS), designed to cater to different storage needs within cloud environments.

Amazon EFS:

Amazon Elastic File System (EFS) is designed to provide serverless, fully elastic file storage that lets you share file data without provisioning or managing storage capacity and performance. You can use an EFS file system as a common data source for workloads and applications running on multiple instances.

Amazon EFS supports the Network File System versions 4.0 and 4.1 (NFSv4) protocols when mounting your file systems on Amazon EC2 instances. Mounting your Amazon EFS file system on your Amazon EC2 instance also requires an NFS client that supports your chosen NFSv4 protocol. Amazon EC2 Mac instances running macOS Big Sur only support NFS v4.0. Amazon EFS is not supported on Windows instances.

Amazon Fsx for Windows File Server:

Amazon FSx for Windows File Server provides fully managed Microsoft Windows file servers backed by a fully native Windows file system. It is primarily used for enterprise workloads. Amazon FSx has native support for Windows file system features and for the industry-standard Server Message Block (SMB) protocol (versions 2.0 to 3.1.1) to access file storage over a network.

Amazon FSx works with Microsoft Active Directory to perform user authentication and access control. You join your Amazon FSx file system to a Microsoft Active Directory while creating it. To create an Amazon FSx file system, you need an Amazon EC2 instance running Microsoft Windows Server in the virtual private cloud (VPC) based on the Amazon VPC service that you want to associate with your Amazon FSx file system.

Amazon Fsx for Lustre:

Amazon FSx for Lustre makes it easy and cost-effective to launch and run the popular, high-performance Lustre file system. You use Lustre for workloads where speed matters, such as machine learning, high-performance computing (HPC), video processing, and financial modeling.

Amzon Fsx for Lustre is designed for Linux-based instances. To create an Amazon FSx for Lustre file system and mount it to your instances, you would need an Amazon EC2 instance running a supported Linux release in your virtual private cloud (VPC) based on the Amazon VPC service. You will install the Lustre client on this EC2 instance, and then mount your FSx for Lustre file system on the EC2 instance. The Lustre client supports Amazon Linux, Amazon Linux 2, CentOS and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.7 through 7.9 and 8.2 through 8.8, Rocky Linux 8.4 through 8.8, SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 12 SP3, SP4, and SP5, and Ubuntu 16.04, 18.04, 20.04, and 22.04.

From the scenario, the company is using Windows-based EC2 instances and already has an existing AWS Managed Microsoft Active Directory. With these details, we know that the most appropriate file system that we can use is Amazon FSx for Windows File Server because it is the only one that supports integration with Microsoft AD and is compatible with Windows-based instances. The SysOps administrator is having difficulties mounting to the EFS file share because it is not compatible with Windows-based instances in the first place. We must reimplement the file system and use Amazon FSx for Windows File Server instead.

Hence, the correct answer is: Remove the EFS file share. Establish a file share for the EC2 instances using Amazon FSx for Windows File Server.

The option that says: Remove the EFS file share. Establish a file share for the EC2 instances using Amazon FSx for Lustre is incorrect because Amazon FSx for Lustre is not compatible with Windows-based instances. As explained above, Amazon FSx for Lustre is used for workloads where speed matters, such as machine learning, high-performance computing (HPC), video processing, and financial modeling. However, it only supports Linux-based instances. It is also important to note that the company already has an established AWS Managed Microsoft AD. The Amazon FSx for Lustre is not capable of integrating with Microsoft AD.

The option that says: Set up the security groups and network ACL to allow NFS port (TCP port 2049) is incorrect. While it is true that you need to allow NFS ports in security groups and NACLs to utilize the EFS properly, however, the Amazon EFS does not support Windows-based instances. Hence, even though it is important to check this configuration, performing this step will not really resolve the issue because the problem persists that the EFS will not work with Windows-based instances.

The option that says: Set up support for NFSv4.1 and install the NFS client to the Amazon EC2 instances is incorrect. Just like the previous option, even though this step is crucial to mount the EFS with an instance, it still will not resolve the issue because EFS does not support Windows-based instances in the first place. EFS supporting NFSv4 ensures compatibility with a wide range of NFSv4-capable clients and enables users to leverage this version’s security and performance benefits. The AWS recommends the use of NFSv4.1. In order to mount the EFS file system to the instance, the instance must have an NFS client installed. These steps are crucial for mounting the EFS file system to EC2 instances supported by the EFS, but they will not help to resolve the issue.

questions.question:
A company runs several Amazon EC2 instances. Most of these instances are deployed by the users in the Development team. However, from the recent reviews, it turns out that most EC2 instances often have more disk performance capacity than is required for the workloads. The CloudOps Engineer, recognizing the need to optimize costs and enhance operational efficiency, must review all Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes associated with the Development team’s EC2 instances. The goal is to create recommendations based on Input/Output Operations Per Second (IOPS) and throughput.

What is the MOST operationally efficient solution the CloudOps Engineer must implement to satisfy the requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service provides IOPS and throughput recommendations for right-sizing over-provisioned EBS volumes?
choices: In each EC2 instance, install the Flexible I/O Tester (fio) tool. Create a configuration file to simulate the required workloads. Perform benchmarking. Compare the benchmark results with EBS volume characteristics.
Utilize AWS Trusted Advisor. In the Trusted Advisor dashboard, check the Cost Optimization tab. Search the recommendations for Amazon EBS over-provisioned volumes check.
In AWS Compute Optimizer, opt in to your account. Wait up to 24 hours. Review the AWS Compute Optimizer findings from the dashboard.
Utilize AWS Service Catalog. Create a product that will deploy instances configured with EBS-optimized instance type. Grant the Development team's users to the portfolio.
correctAnswers: In AWS Compute Optimizer, opt in to your account. Wait up to 24 hours. Review the AWS Compute Optimizer findings from the dashboard.
questions.explanation:
AWS Compute Optimizer is a service that analyzes the configuration and utilization metrics of your AWS resources. It reports whether your resources are optimal, and generates optimization recommendations to reduce the cost and improve the performance of your workloads. Compute Optimizer also provides graphs showing recent utilization metric history data, as well as projected utilization for recommendations, which you can use to evaluate which recommendation provides the best price-performance trade-off. The analysis and visualization of your usage patterns can help you decide when to move or resize your running resources, and still meet your performance and capacity requirements.

Compute Optimizer delivers intuitive and easily actionable EBS volume recommendations so that you can identify optimal provisioned IOPS settings and volume sizes for your EBS volumes based on your performance needs, without requiring specialized knowledge or investing substantial time and money.

You must opt in to have Compute Optimizer analyze your AWS resources. The service supports standalone AWS accounts, member accounts of an organization, and the management account of an organization.

After you opt-in, you’re redirected to the dashboard in the Compute Optimizer console. At the same time, the service immediately starts analyzing the configuration and utilization metrics of your AWS resources. Findings and optimization recommendations can take up to 24 hours to be generated. Moreover, for optimization recommendations can be generated, at least 30 consecutive hours of metric data must be accumulated for Amazon EBS Volumes.

The EBS volumes recommendations page lists each of your current volumes, their finding classifications, their current volume type, and the current hourly price. From there, you can find the EBS volume details page, which lists up to three optimization recommendations for a specific volume. It lists the specifications for each recommendation, their performance risk, and their monthly prices. Furthermore, the details page displays utilization metric graphs for the current volume.

With AWS Compute Optimizer, the SysOps administrator can review all Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes associated with the Development team’s EC2 instances. It can give recommendations that will help the administrator identify the most suitable provisioned IOPS and volume sizes for the required workload.

Hence, the correct answer is: In AWS Compute Optimizer, opt in to your account. Wait up to 24 hours. Review the AWS Compute Optimizer findings from the dashboard.

The option that says: Utilize AWS Service Catalog. Create a product that will deploy instances configured with EBS-optimized instance type. Grant the Development team’s users to the portfolio is incorrect. While EBS-optimized instances can improve EBS volume performance by reducing contention with other EC2 instance traffic, it does not tell whether an EBS volume is over-provisioned or sufficient for a given workload. The problem of EBS volumes having more disk performance capacity than required still persists.

The option that says: In each EC2 instance, install the Flexible I/O Tester (fio) tool. Create a configuration file to simulate the required workloads. Perform benchmarking. Compare the benchmark results with EBS volume characteristics is incorrect. Although this solution can give the SysOps administrator details on what EBS volume characteristics are optimal for particular workloads, it is less operationally inefficient. Note that there are already existing instances, and the scenario did not mention how many and whether each has the same workload. To implement this solution, the administrator must do this to each EC2 instance. The fio tool lets you simulate the required workload to the instance or benchmarking. The benchmark results will give details on whether the attached volume is optimal for the workload.

The option that says: Utilize AWS Trusted Advisor. In the Trusted Advisor dashboard, check the Cost Optimization tab. Search the recommendations for Amazon EBS over-provisioned volumes check is incorrect. Even though it is true that Trusted Advisor can provide recommendations for Amazon EBS overprovisioned and underutilized volumes, it is still inadequate as it does not offer the same level of specificity as the AWS Compute Optimizer. AWS Trusted Advisor simply gives a broader set of best practices and recommendations across multiple pillars, including cost optimization. On the other hand, AWS Compute Optimizer offers specific recommendations, including instance types and sizes based on historical resource utilization data. This aligns more with the requirement to review instances with more disk performance capacity than needed.

questions.question:
A digital advertising company has a set of S3 buckets that contains various data such as Ad clickthrough rate (CTR), Cost Per Click (CPC) and many others. The analytics management team needs to analyze the data hosted in the S3 bucket using standard SQL to produce reports.

Which of the following AWS services can help meet their demand?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service allows querying data stored in S3 using standard SQL?
choices: Amazon Aurora
Amazon ElastiCache
Amazon RDS
Amazon Athena
correctAnswers: Amazon Athena
questions.explanation:
Amazon Athena is an interactive query service that makes it easy to analyze data in Amazon S3 using standard SQL. Athena is serverless, so there is no infrastructure to manage, and you pay only for the queries that you run.

Athena is easy to use. Simply point to your data in Amazon S3, define the schema, and start querying using standard SQL. Most results are delivered within seconds. With Athena, there’s no need for complex ETL jobs to prepare your data for analysis. This makes it easy for anyone with SQL skills to quickly analyze large-scale datasets.

Hence, the correct answer is: Amazon Athena.

Amazon RDS is incorrect because RDS is used to manage SQL databases.

Amazon Aurora is incorrect because Aurora is a fully managed relational database engine that’s compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL.

Amazon ElastiCache is incorrect because ElastiCache is primarily used as an in-memory caching service to improve database performance. The scenario is asking for a service to use to analyze the data hosted in the S3 bucket using standard SQL to produce reports.

questions.question: A blockchain wallet service is hosted on an On-Demand EC2 instance in your VPC. You produced a custom AMI from it as you have to share it to the other AWS account that your company also owns in another region. Which of the following is the right way to ensure that the other account has access to the AMI?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you securely share a custom AMI with a specific AWS account in another region?
choices: Upload and sell the AMI in the AWS Marketplace.
Make the custom AMI public.
Share the AMI with the specific AWS account.
Upload the AMI in a public S3 bucket.
correctAnswers: Share the AMI with the specific AWS account.
questions.explanation:
You can share an AMI with specific AWS accounts without making the AMI public. All you need are the AWS account IDs. AMIs are a regional resource. Therefore, sharing an AMI makes it available in that region. To make an AMI available in a different region, copy the AMI to the region and then share it. There is no limit to the number of AWS accounts with which an AMI can be shared.

Take note that you cannot directly share an AMI that contains a snapshot of an encrypted volume. You can share your encrypted snapshots with other AWS accounts. This enables the other account to copy the snapshots to other regions, re-encrypt the snapshots, and create AMIs using the encrypted snapshots.

Hence, the correct answer is: Share the AMI with the specific AWS account.

The option that says: Make the custom AMI public is incorrect because the AMI should only be used by members of the company and should not be available for public use.

The option that says: Upload and sell the AMI in the AWS Marketplace is incorrect because this will make the AMI public. The AMI should only be shared to another AWS account that belongs to your company.

The option that says: Upload the AMI in a public S3 bucket is incorrect because you don’t need the AMI to be in a public bucket. You need to make sure that your AMI can only be accessed by a specific AWS account in your organization and should not be available to the public.

questions.question:
A company runs Linux-based Amazon EC2 instances backed by Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes. These EC2 instances are often started and stopped. When EC2 instances are started, EBS volumes restored from snapshots are attached to them. However, the restored EBS volumes initially exhibit lower performance than required, affecting the workload due to the need for almost all the provisioned IOPS. A solution is necessary to ensure EBS volumes deliver the expected performance right after being restored from snapshots.

Which of the following solutions would meet this requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which EBS feature ensures full performance immediately after restoring a volume from a snapshot?
choices: Set the Linux read-ahead buffer to 1 MiB on the EC2 instances.
Increase the size of the EBS volumes.
Allocate each snapshot to an unencrypted EBS volume. Once the performance stabilizes, proceed to encrypt the EBS volume.
Enable Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR) for the snapshots.
correctAnswers: Enable Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR) for the snapshots.
questions.explanation:
Amazon EBS Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR) enables the immediate provision of the full performance of an Amazon EBS volume restored from a snapshot. When enabled, FSR ensures that the EBS volumes deliver their expected IOPS and throughput right after they are created from the snapshots, eliminating the performance lag associated with the lazy loading of data from snapshots. This feature is particularly beneficial for workloads that require consistent and predictable performance immediately after the volume is available.

FSR is designed to address the scenario described, where volumes restored from snapshots need to meet high-performance demands immediately. By pre-warming the data blocks of the snapshots, FSR allows EBS volumes to perform at the required level without the initial delay typically seen with standard snapshot restores.

Hence, the correct answer is: Enable Fast Snapshot Restore (FSR) for the snapshots.

The option that says: Allocate each snapshot to an unencrypted EBS volume. Once the performance stabilizes, proceed to encrypt the EBS volume is incorrect because it simply does not address the initial low performance of restored EBS volumes. Encryption’s impact on performance is minimal due to AWS’s hardware acceleration, making this step unnecessary and not a solution to the performance problem.

The option that says: Increase the size of the EBS volumes is incorrect because increasing volume size only boosts baseline performance and does not specifically solve the initial performance dip following a snapshot restore. This option might improve overall performance but doesn’t address the core issue of snapshot restoration performance.

The option that says: Set the Linux read-ahead buffer to 1 MiB on the EC2 instances is incorrect because it primarily optimizes sequential read performance and does not directly address the challenge of enhancing the initial performance of EBS volumes after they are restored from snapshots. It’s a general tweak that enhances sequential reads but doesn’t directly enhance the startup performance of restored volumes, which is essential in this scenario.

questions.question:
A retail company suffered a cache failure during a high-traffic sales period, causing widespread application downtime. Following a post-incident review, the engineering team must redesign the existing Amazon ElastiCache (Redis OSS) deployment, which is used to cache application data, so it can recover automatically from infrastructure failures without manual intervention. The redesigned architecture must also minimize the recovery time objective (RTO).

Which configuration change would the engineering team implement to satisfy both requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which ElastiCache configuration enables automatic failover and minimizes RTO for a Redis replication group?
choices: Configure Amazon Route 53 health checks to monitor the ElastiCache primary node and automatically reroute traffic to a standby cluster in a second Availability Zone.
Create an Amazon EventBridge rule to initiate an ElastiCache snapshot every hour and restore from the most recent snapshot after a failure occurs.
Enable Multi-AZ on the ElastiCache (Redis OSS) replication group and provision a read replica in a second Availability Zone.
Replace the existing ElastiCache (Redis OSS) deployment with Amazon MemoryDB for Redis to leverage its Multi-AZ transactional log for stronger data durability.
correctAnswers: Enable Multi-AZ on the ElastiCache (Redis OSS) replication group and provision a read replica in a second Availability Zone.
questions.explanation:
When Multi-AZ is enabled on an Amazon ElastiCache (Redis OSS) replication group, ElastiCache automatically promotes a read replica to primary if the primary node becomes unavailable. Provisioning that replica in a separate Availability Zone ensures the promotion can succeed even during localized infrastructure failures, directly addressing both the resilience requirement and the need to minimize the recovery time objective (RTO) for the application data cache.

Unlike backup-and-restore approaches, Multi-AZ failover in ElastiCache is automatic and typically completes within seconds, making it simply the most effective way to reduce RTO. The DNS endpoint of the replication group is updated to point to the promoted replica, so applications reconnect without manual intervention. A replication group must have at least one read replica, and Multi-AZ must be explicitly enabled — simply creating a replica is not enough to activate automatic failover on its own.

Modifying Redis OSS cache to enable Multi AZ

Key behaviors of ElastiCache (Redis OSS) Multi-AZ:

– Automatic failover promotes only the replica with the least replication lag, minimizing data loss and ensuring the cache returns to service as quickly as possible.

– After failover, ElastiCache automatically creates a replacement replica in the original primary’s Availability Zone, restoring full redundancy without any manual action.

– Multi-AZ is available only for Redis OSS replication groups, not for single-node clusters or Memcached clusters.

Solutions that rely on scheduled snapshots or manual restores are simply not suitable when the priority is minimizing RTO, because recovery time is bounded only by snapshot frequency and restore duration rather than by automated failover logic.

Hence, the correct answer is: Enable Multi-AZ on the ElastiCache (Redis OSS) replication group and provision a read replica in a second Availability Zone.

The option that says: Create an Amazon EventBridge rule to initiate an ElastiCache snapshot every hour and restore from the most recent snapshot after a failure occurs is incorrect. Restoring from a snapshot is just a manual, time-consuming process that cannot match the speed of automated Multi-AZ failover. The RTO remains directly tied to snapshot frequency and restore duration, both of which are far longer than the near-instant promotion of a standby replica. This approach does not satisfy the requirement to minimize RTO and does not provide automatic recovery without manual intervention.

The option that says: Configure Amazon Route 53 health checks to monitor the ElastiCache primary node and automatically reroute traffic to a standby cluster in a second Availability Zone is incorrect. Amazon Route 53 health checks are primarily designed for DNS-level traffic routing between endpoints and are not a mechanism for ElastiCache node failover. ElastiCache replication groups do not expose individual node endpoints through Route 53 in a way that supports this pattern. This approach introduces unnecessary complexity and still does not provide the automatic in-cluster failover that Multi-AZ with a read replica delivers natively.

The option that says: Replace the existing ElastiCache (Redis OSS) deployment with Amazon MemoryDB for Redis to leverage its Multi-AZ transactional log for stronger data durability is incorrect. While Amazon MemoryDB for Redis does offer Multi-AZ durability through its distributed transactional log, replacing the entire cache tier with a different service is simply a significant architectural change that goes well beyond a configuration update. The existing ElastiCache (Redis OSS) replication group can already satisfy both the automatic recovery and RTO requirements by enabling Multi-AZ with a read replica, making a full service replacement unnecessary and disproportionate to the problem.

questions.question:
A financial services company is preparing for a mandatory regulatory audit that requires documented evidence of how application errors are classified across its serverless platform. The company runs dozens of AWS Lambda functions, with each function writing logs to a dedicated Amazon CloudWatch Logs log group. The compliance team needs a consolidated report showing the count of processing failures, broken down by error type, across every log group.

Which approach should a CloudOps engineer take to generate this report with the fewest additional steps?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How can you query and count Lambda errors by type across multiple CloudWatch Logs log groups efficiently?
choices: Run a multi-log-group query in CloudWatch Logs Insights using the stats command and count function to tally errors by type.
Enable AWS X-Ray tracing on each Lambda function and use the X-Ray Analytics console to count and group application errors by type.
Enable CloudWatch Contributor Insights rules on each log group to report on the top error types per Lambda function.
Export all Lambda log data to an Amazon S3 bucket, then run an Amazon Athena query with SELECT and GROUP BY keywords to count errors.
correctAnswers: Run a multi-log-group query in CloudWatch Logs Insights using the stats command and count function to tally errors by type.
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights is a fully managed, interactive query service that allows engineers to analyze log data stored across one or more CloudWatch Logs log groups without needing to export data or provision any infrastructure. Logs Insights supports a purpose-built query language that includes the stats command, which performs aggregations such as counts, averages, and sums over matched log events. When combined with the count() function and a by clause, it produces a grouped summary of event occurrences directly within the CloudWatch console or via the AWS CLI.

One of the most operationally efficient features of Logs Insights is its ability to query multiple log groups simultaneously in a single request. A CloudOps engineer can specify all relevant Lambda log groups in the query scope, apply a filter pattern that isolates error events, and then use stats count(*) by errorType to aggregate failure counts by category. This approach requires no data movement, no additional services, and no infrastructure setup, making it the most direct solution for the compliance team’s requirement.

CloudWatch Logs Insights query

Logs Insights supports several aggregation functions that are useful for compliance and audit reporting scenarios:

– count() returns the total number of matching log events, optionally grouped by a field value.

– stats is the command used to compute aggregate statistics over query results, such as grouping error events by their type field.

– Queries can target multiple log groups in a single execution, which is essential when Lambda functions each write to their own log group.

Solutions that require exporting data to Amazon S3, enabling per-log-group rules, or instrumenting functions with a separate tracing service introduce unnecessary operational overhead and are not the appropriate fit when logs already reside in CloudWatch Logs.

Hence, the correct answer is: Run a multi-log-group query in CloudWatch Logs Insights using the stats command and count function to tally errors by type.

The option that says: Enable CloudWatch Contributor Insights rules on each log group to report on the top error types per Lambda function is incorrect. Amazon CloudWatch Contributor Insights analyzes log data to identify the top contributors to system performance metrics, such as the highest-volume callers or most frequent IP addresses. It does not simply provide a flexible aggregation query interface for counting and grouping arbitrary error types across multiple log groups, and it requires individual rule configuration per log group rather than a single unified query.

The option that says: Export all Lambda log data to an Amazon S3 bucket, then run an Amazon Athena query with SELECT and GROUP BY keywords to count errors is incorrect. While Amazon Athena can query structured data in S3 using standard SQL, this approach requires setting up a log export pipeline from CloudWatch Logs to S3, configuring an Athena table schema, and managing the export process. This introduces significant operational overhead compared to simply running a native Logs Insights query that operates directly on existing log data.

The option that says: Enable AWS X-Ray tracing on each Lambda function and use the X-Ray Analytics console to count and group application errors by type is incorrect. AWS X-Ray is a distributed tracing service designed to track requests as they flow through application components, helping engineers identify latency bottlenecks and faults in service maps. It does not aggregate or group log-level error entries written to CloudWatch Logs log groups, and its Analytics console is built for trace filtering rather than structured error counting across multiple log sources.

questions.question:
A leading commercial bank is modernizing its infrastructure and optimizing application deployment using cloud-native technologies. To leverage the benefits of the cloud, the bank creates an Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (Amazon EKS) cluster with AWS Fargate, a serverless compute engine for containers, eliminating the need for manual infrastructure management.

After the successful deployment of the EKS cluster, the bank’s team needs to manage and interact with the cluster’s API server using the kubectl command line tool.

As a CloudOps Engineer, which of the following should you implement so that kubectl can communicate with the cluster API server?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service enables kubectl to communicate with an Amazon EKS cluster API server?
choices: AWS OpsWorks Chef Automate
EC2 Dashboard
AWS Systems Manager Automate
AWS Trusted Advisor
correctAnswers: AWS Systems Manager Automate
questions.explanation:
AWS Systems Manager Automate is the correct answer. Systems Manager Automation is an AWS-hosted service that simplifies common instance and system maintenance and deployment tasks. Automation offers one-click automations for simplifying complex tasks such as creating golden Amazon Machines Images (AMIs), and recovering unreachable EC2 instances.

AWS Trusted Advisor is incorrect because it is just an online resource to help you reduce cost, increase performance, and improve security by optimizing your AWS environment.

EC2 Dashboard is incorrect because it will just give information and services that you can use with EC2.

AWS OpsWorks Chef Automate is incorrect because it is a suite of automation tools from Chef mainly used for configuration management, compliance and security, and continuous deployment.

questions.question:
A company manages a multi-account environment for resource isolation. A critical application is dependent on a Lambda function deployed in the PROD account. This function requires permission to query data stored in a DynamoDB table in the DATA account.

The CloudOps Engineer must grant the Lambda function access to the DynamoDB table in the DATA account from the PROD account.

What is the MOST secure solution that will satisfy the requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How should a Lambda function in one AWS account securely access a DynamoDB table in a different account?
choices: In the DATA account, generate programmatic access that allows query actions to the DynamoDB table. Store the generated credentials in the Lambda function's environment variables. Configure the Lambda code to reference the environment variables.

In the DATA account, configure an IAM role that allows query actions from the Lambda IAM role. Modify the policy attached in Lambda’s IAM role to allow iam:PassRole and iam:GetRole permissions to the DynamoDB service, and the DynamoDB table resource.

In the DATA account, set up an IAM role that allows query access in the DynamoDB table. Configure a trust policy in the newly created IAM role to include the Lambda IAM role. Modify the policy attached in Lambda’s IAM role to allow sts:AssumeRole permission to the IAM role from the DATA account.

In the DATA account, attach a resource-based policy in the DynamoDB table. Configure the resource-based policy to allow query actions from the Lambda IAM role. Modify the policy attached in Lambda’s IAM role to allow dynamoDB:Query permission to the DynamoDB table.
correctAnswers: In the DATA account, set up an IAM role that allows query access in the DynamoDB table. Configure a trust policy in the newly created IAM role to include the Lambda IAM role. Modify the policy attached in Lambda’s IAM role to allow sts:AssumeRole permission to the IAM role from the DATA account.
questions.explanation:
An IAM role is an identity within your AWS account that has specific permissions. It is similar to an IAM user, but is not associated with a specific person. You can temporarily assume an IAM role in the AWS Management Console by switching roles. You can assume a role by calling an AWS CLI or AWS API operation or by using a custom URL.

You can use an IAM role to allow someone (a trusted principal) in a different account to access resources in your account. Roles are the primary way to grant cross-account access. However, with some AWS services, you can attach a policy directly to a resource (instead of using a role as a proxy).

You can configure cross-account access to Amazon DynamoDB using the following steps:

1. Create a role in the DATA account – This role will be used by the PROD account to gain permissions to access the DATA account.

2. Add a trust policy in the created role in the DATA account to allow Lambda’s IAM role.

2. Create a policy to access the DATA account in the PROD account. Paste the following JSON document:

3. Attach the policy to the Lambda IAM role in the PROD account.

To assume an IAM role in another account you need to utilize a trust policy. A trust policy defines which principals can assume the role, and under which conditions. A trust policy is a specific type of resource-based policy for IAM roles. A role can have only one trust policy.

With these steps, Lambda’s IAM role can access the DynamoDB table in the DATA account and perform query operations.

Hence, the correct answer is: In the DATA account, set up an IAM role that allows query access in the DynamoDB table. Configure a trust policy in the newly created IAM role to include the Lambda IAM role. Modify the policy attached in Lambda’s IAM role to allow sts:AssumeRole permission to the IAM role from the DATA account.

The option that says: In the DATA account, configure an IAM role that allows query actions from the Lambda IAM role. Modify the policy attached in Lambda’s IAM role to allow iam:PassRole and iam:GetRole permissions to the DynamoDB service and DynamoDB table resource is incorrect because this solution will not work. Utilizing iam:PassRole and iam:GetRole is not appropriate for this scenario. PassRole simply controls which IAM roles a given IAM user or IAM role can pass to a service. It is not for giving access to the IAM roles from another account.

The option that says: In the DATA account, attach a resource-based policy in the DynamoDB table. Configure the resource-based policy to allow query actions from the Lambda IAM role. Modify the policy attached in Lambda’s IAM role to allow dynamoDB:Query permission to the DynamoDB table is incorrect. Even though it is true that using resource-based policies can allow cross-account access, however, DynamoDB does not support resource-based policies. Resource-based policies are policies attached directly to a resource. With resource-based policies, you can specify who has access to the resource and what actions they can perform on it.

The option that says: In the DATA account, generate programmatic access that allows query actions to the DynamoDB table. Store the generated credentials in the Lambda function’s environment variables. Configure the Lambda code to reference the environment variables is incorrect. While this solution can allow the Lambda IAM role access to the DynamoDB table in the DATA account, it’s less secure since sensitive information in environment variables can be viewed in plaintext. Moreover, it is not a best practice to generate IAM user credentials. These credentials are not temporary, and once obtained by unintended parties, they can compromise the security of the AWS environment.

questions.question:
A company is utilizing Amazon EC2 instances to host multiple applications. Each instance is patched using the AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager and tagged with a corresponding project code. One of the projects requires a consistent and preset CloudWatch Agent configuration; however, the project’s Amazon EC2 instances are hosted on different AWS regions.

What is the MOST operationally efficient way solution that the CloudOps Engineer must do to satisfy the project requirement? (Select TWO)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you efficiently deploy a consistent CloudWatch Agent configuration across EC2 instances in multiple regions?
choices: Execute the AmazonCloudWatch-ManageAgent Systems Manager Run Command document by configuring a Systems Manager State Manager association. Choose "ssm" as an optional configuration source. Specify instance tags on target selection.
Save the configuration file of the CloudWatch agent in a centralized Amazon S3 bucket.
Save the CloudWatch agent configuration file contents in the Systems Manager Parameter Store
Execute a custom Systems Manager Run Command document by configuring a Systems Manager State Manager association. The document will copy the configuration file from the S3 bucket to the instance.
Save the CloudWatch agent configuration file contents in the Systems Manager OpsCenter.
correctAnswers: Save the configuration file of the CloudWatch agent in a centralized Amazon S3 bucket.
Execute a custom Systems Manager Run Command document by configuring a Systems Manager State Manager association. The document will copy the configuration file from the S3 bucket to the instance.
questions.explanation:
The AWS recommends that you create a standard Amazon CloudWatch agent configuration that includes the system logs and metrics that you want to capture across all your Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) instances and on-premises servers. You can use the CloudWatch agent configuration file wizard to help you create the configuration file.

The CloudWatch agent configuration file can be stored in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store parameters. You can create separate Parameter Store parameters if you have multiple CloudWatch agent configuration files. If you are using multiple AWS accounts or AWS Regions, you must manage and update the Parameter Store parameters in each account and Region. Alternatively, you can centrally manage your CloudWatch configurations as files in Amazon S3 or a version-control tool of your choice.

By default, CloudWatch configurations can be stored and applied as Parameter Store parameters or as CloudWatch configuration files. Using Parameter Store parameters to manage CloudWatch configurations works well if you have a single, standard CloudWatch agent configuration file that you want to apply and manage in a small set of AWS accounts and Regions.

However, using Parameter Store parameters to manage CloudWatch configurations works well if you have a single, standard CloudWatch agent configuration file that you want to apply and manage in a small set of AWS accounts and Regions. Using this approach can face some challenges:

– If you use multiple Regions, you must synchronize CloudWatch configuration updates in each Region’s Parameter Store.

– If you have multiple CloudWatch configurations, you must initiate the retrieval and application of each Parameter Store configuration.

– If you use multiple accounts, you must ensure that each new account has the required CloudWatch configurations in its Parameter Store.

To address these challenges, you can store CloudWatch configurations in an S3 bucket that is accessible from all your accounts and Regions instead. You can then copy these configurations from the S3 bucket to the CloudWatch configuration directory by using Systems Manager Automation runbooks and Systems Manager State Manager.

These are the two main actions that you must perform:

1. Create Systems Manager custom documents

2. Create State Manager associations

The scenario did not specify if the preset configuration would be applied only to a small set of regions. Therefore, using the Systems Manager Parameter Store is not a suitable approach. Using a centralized location like an S3 bucket would be a better approach.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Save the configuration file of the CloudWatch agent in a centralized Amazon S3 bucket.

– Execute a custom Systems Manager Run Command document by configuring a Systems Manager State Manager association. The document will copy the configuration file from the S3 bucket to the instance.

The option that says: Save the CloudWatch agent configuration file contents in the Systems Manager OpsCenter is incorrect because Systems Manager OpsCenter is primarily used for incident response and management. It is designed to help users manage and resolve operational issues, incidents, and investigations rather than storing configuration files. Therefore, saving the CloudWatch agent configuration file contents directly in Systems Manager OpsCenter is not a typical or intended use case for OpsCenter.

The option that says: Save the CloudWatch agent configuration file contents in the Systems Manager Parameter Store is incorrect. Although this resolves the issue, a significant operational overhead remains; thus, this implementation isn’t enough. As explained above, for the multiple regions set up, you must synchronize CloudWatch configuration updates in each Region’s Parameter Store. Parameter Store is a Regional service, and the same parameter must be updated in each Region that uses the CloudWatch agent.

The option that says: Execute the AmazonCloudWatch-ManageAgent Systems Manager Run Command document by configuring a Systems Manager State Manager association. Choose “ssm” as an optional configuration source. Specify instance tags on target selection is incorrect. Just like the other incorrect option, this step utilizes the Systems Manager Parameter Store. This step completes the solution using the default approach of installing the Cloudwatch Agent to multiple instances using the Systems Manager Parameter Store. AmazonCloudWatch-ManageAgent is a preconfigured SSM document that will maintain the CloudWatch agent configuration for each EC2 instance based on the configuration file stored in the Parameter Store. However, with this solution, you would have to synchronize the parameter store for each Region because the parameter store is regional. It is not the appropriate solution for the requirement.

questions.question:
A broadcasting company runs a widely used multimedia platform on AWS. This platform uses an Amazon DynamoDB table with provisioned capacity to index videos stored in an Amazon S3 bucket. A notable event recently drew an enormous audience, causing read request throttling at the DynamoDB table, which in turn disrupted video display on the platform.

To address the immediate issue, the operations team temporarily enhanced the capacity of the DynamoDB table to accommodate the surge in traffic. To be alerted before throttling occurs, the organization has set up an Amazon SNS topic with subscriptions to the operations team’s email.

Which action should be taken to meet the requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which CloudWatch alarm metric proactively alerts before DynamoDB read throttling occurs?
choices: Set Amazon CloudWatch Logs as the destination for the application logs. Create a metric filter to monitor for ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors and trigger an SNS topic notification.
Configure DynamoDB Streams to monitor THROTTLING_EXCEPTION status code events. Use AWS Lambda to process these stream records and send alerts to the SNS topic.

Create an Amazon CloudWatch alarm based on the ConsumedReadCapacityUnits metric for the DynamoDB table. Set a threshold value that is near the DynamoDB table’s provisioned capacity and set the alarm to notify the SNS topic.
Enable DynamoDB auto-scaling to adjust the table's capacity based on usage. Set up an Amazon EventBridge rule to alert the SNS topic during scaling events.
correctAnswers: Create an Amazon CloudWatch alarm based on the ConsumedReadCapacityUnits metric for the DynamoDB table. Set a threshold value that is near the DynamoDB table’s provisioned capacity and set the alarm to notify the SNS topic.
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudWatch allows you to set alarms that notify you via Amazon SNS when specified metrics exceed or fall below predefined criteria. These alarms monitor specified metrics over a designated timeframe, taking action when the metric’s value crosses a set threshold consistently over a predetermined number of evaluation periods. The action usually entails sending a notification via Amazon SNS or triggering an Auto Scaling policy. It’s important to note that these alarms are designed to respond to changes that persist for a specific duration, not just any momentary fluctuation in the state. Therefore, an alarm triggers not merely because a metric reaches a particular state but because it has remained there long enough to indicate a significant and sustained change in conditions.

ConsumedReadCapacityUnits metric

Creating a CloudWatch alarm based on the ConsumedReadCapacityUnits metric allows monitoring of the read capacity units that the DynamoDB table consumes. By setting the alarm threshold close to the provisioned capacity limit, the operations team can receive notifications before throttling occurs, thus allowing them to take preemptive actions to adjust the capacity or optimize queries to avoid service interruptions.

Hence, the correct answer is: Create an Amazon CloudWatch alarm based on the ConsumedReadCapacityUnits metric for the DynamoDB table. Set a threshold value that is near the DynamoDB table’s provisioned capacity and set the alarm to notify the SNS topic.

The option that says: Enable DynamoDB auto-scaling to adjust the table’s capacity based on usage. Set up an Amazon EventBridge rule to alert the SNS topic during scaling events is incorrect. While enabling auto scaling can help manage capacity dynamically, it does not provide proactive alerts before throttling occurs. The EventBridge rule would notify only after scaling events.

The option that says: Configure DynamoDB Streams to monitor THROTTLING_EXCEPTION status code events. Use AWS Lambda to process these stream records and send alerts to the SNS topic is incorrect. DynamoDB Streams simply captures time-ordered sequence of item-level modifications in a DynamoDB table. It’s not typically used for monitoring performance metrics like error events.

The option that says: Set Amazon CloudWatch Logs as the destination for the application logs. Create a metric filter to monitor for ProvisionedThroughputExceededException errors and trigger an SNS topic notification is incorrect. This approach would only react to throttling after it has occurred. Thus, it fails to meet the proactive alerting requirement, as it notifies only once the application has been negatively affected by throttling.

questions.question:
A corporation must set up its on-premises DNS server to access DNS records foragila.pinas.com from a private Route 53 hosted zone. The corporation has already established a direct connection to its Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) through AWS Direct Connect. A Cloud Engineer is tasked with implementing a solution to enable an on-premises server to resolve agila.pinas.com domain records.

What actions should the engineer take to fulfill these requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which Route 53 Resolver endpoint allows on-premises DNS servers to resolve records in a private hosted zone?
choices: Establish an inbound endpoint on Amazon Route 53 Resolver. Add a security group that allows outbound traffic for TCP/UDP port 53 towards the on-premises DNS server IP address.
Set up an inbound endpoint on Amazon Route 53 Resolver. Configure a security group for the endpoint that permits incoming traffic on TCP/UDP port 53 from the on-premises DNS server IP address.

Set up an outbound endpoint in Route 53 Resolver. Link a security group with the endpoint to enable the sending of outbound requests through TCP/UDP port 53 toward the on-premises DNS server IP address.

Set up an Amazon Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint and link it with a security group. The security group should permit incoming traffic on TCP/UDP port 53 exclusively from the on-premises DNS server IP address.
correctAnswers: Set up an inbound endpoint on Amazon Route 53 Resolver. Configure a security group for the endpoint that permits incoming traffic on TCP/UDP port 53 from the on-premises DNS server IP address.
questions.explanation:
Amazon Route 53 Resolver helps resolve domain name system (DNS) queries between on-premises networks and AWS Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs). This service enables seamless routing of DNS queries, which is crucial for enterprises using AWS and on-premises resources. The Route 53 Resolver has two types of endpoints: inbound and outbound endpoints. Inbound endpoints route DNS queries from on-premises networks to AWS, while outbound endpoints manage queries from AWS to the on-premises DNS servers.

Organizations that use AWS Direct Connect or VPN connections can benefit from setting up a Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint. This allows their on-premises DNS resolvers to send DNS queries to AWS for domains hosted in Route 53 privately hosted zones. This setup is crucial for efficiently and securely resolving DNS queries for applications distributed across on-premises data centers and AWS. The security groups associated with these endpoints can be configured to control traffic based on the TCP and UDP port 53 to ensure that only legitimate DNS traffic is allowed between these networks.

Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoints are essential for enhancing DNS query resolution reliability and ensuring network architecture remains robust against potential DNS-related security threats. By managing DNS queries centrally through Route 53, organizations can achieve more consistent performance and reduce complexity in their DNS architectures. Therefore, Amazon Route 53 Resolver is vital for enterprises looking to streamline their hybrid cloud DNS management.

Hence, the correct answer is: Set up an inbound endpoint on Amazon Route 53 Resolver. Configure a security group for the endpoint that permits incoming traffic on TCP/UDP port 53 from the on-premises DNS server IP address.

The option that says: Establish an inbound endpoint on Amazon Route 53 Resolver. Add a security group that allows outbound traffic for TCP/UDP port 53 towards the on-premises DNS server IP address is incorrect. Using an inbound endpoint for handling DNS queries from the on-premises network is correct. However, security group’s configuration suggests allowing outbound traffic from the VPC to the on-premises DNS server, which is not applicable for the scenario where the on-premises server needs to resolve DNS queries using the VPC DNS resolver.

The option that says: Set up an Amazon Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint and link it with a security group. The security group should permit incoming traffic on TCP/UDP port 53 exclusively from the on-premises DNS server IP address is incorrect. Outbound endpoints are primarily designed for resolving DNS queries originating within AWS to external (non-AWS) networks, not for receiving DNS queries from an on-premises server.

The option that says: Set up an outbound endpoint in Route 53 Resolver. Link a security group with the endpoint to enable the sending of outbound requests through TCP/UDP port 53 toward the on-premises DNS server IP address is incorrect because it pertains to outbound DNS queries from the VPC rather than handling inbound queries from the on-premises server.

questions.question:
A corporation’s CloudOps team needs to guarantee that each Amazon EC2 Windows instance launched within the AWS environment contains specific custom software. This software comes in the form of an.msi installer.

The organization utilizes AWS Systems Manager for software updates, and the Windows instances carry distinct tags. The custom software occasionally receives updates, and the CloudOps team must implement these updates without manual intervention.

What sequence of actions would fulfill these criteria with MINIMAL administrative effort? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which two Systems Manager steps automatically deploy and update custom MSI software on tagged Windows EC2 instances?
choices: Set up the Systems Manager Distributor package for the custom software.
Attach an IAM role with the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy to all Windows EC2 instances to enable Systems Manager to manage and deploy software packages.

Set up an OpsItem in Systems Manager associated with the Windows tag value. Connect the Distributor package in Systems Manager to this OpsItem. Design a specific maintenance period for the package's deployment and ensure this period extends throughout the day.

Set up a State Manager association in Systems Manager to execute the AWS-ConfigureAWSPackage document. Input the specifics of the custom software package. Choose the instance tags in line with the Windows server tag with a schedule of 1 week

Set up a State Manager association in Systems Manager to execute the AWS-RunRemoteScript document. Input the specifics of the custom software package. Choose the instance tags in line with the Windows server tag with a schedule of 1 week
correctAnswers: Set up the Systems Manager Distributor package for the custom software.

Set up a State Manager association in Systems Manager to execute the AWS-ConfigureAWSPackage document. Input the specifics of the custom software package. Choose the instance tags in line with the Windows server tag with a schedule of 1 week
questions.explanation:
AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager automates the process of patching managed instances with security-related updates. For Linux-based instances, you can also install patches for non-security updates. You can patch fleets of Amazon EC2 instances or your on-premises servers and virtual machines (VMs) by operating system type.

This includes supported versions of Windows, Ubuntu Server, Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL), SUSE Linux Enterprise Server (SLES), Amazon Linux, and Amazon Linux 2. You can scan instances to see only a report of missing patches, or you can scan and automatically install all missing patches.

AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager

AWS Systems Manager Distributor allows you to securely distribute and install software packages, like your custom software’s.msi installer, across a large set of instances. By creating a Distributor package for your software, you are packaging it in a way that the Systems Manager can then use to distribute and install it on EC2 Windows instances.

AWS Systems Manager State Manager uses associations to enforce a desired state for your instances. By setting up an association to run the AWS-ConfigureAWSPackage document, you’re effectively telling the Systems Manager to install or update the specified package (in this case, your custom software) on instances with the specified tags. Since the software needs occasional updates, using this State Manager association ensures that those updates are applied automatically without the need for manual intervention.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Set up the Systems Manager Distributor package for the custom software.

– Set up a State Manager association in Systems Manager to execute the AWS-ConfigureAWSPackage document. Input the specifics of the custom software package. Choose the instance tags in line with the Windows server tag with a schedule of 1 week.

The option that says: Set up a State Manager association in Systems Manager to execute the AWS-RunRemoteScript document. Input the specifics of the custom software package. Choose the instance tags in line with the Windows server tag with a schedule of 1 week is incorrect. The AWS-RunRemoteScript document is typically used to run scripts on managed instances, typically PowerShell scripts or shell scripts. It is not specialized for deploying or updating software packages.

The option that says: Set up an OpsItem in Systems Manager associated with the Windows tag value. Connect the Distributor package in Systems Manager to this OpsItem. Design a specific maintenance period for the package’s deployment and ensure this period extends throughout the day is incorrect because creating an OpsItem is more about operational work items or tasks that need to be addressed by the Ops team.

The option that says: Attach an IAM role with the AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore policy to all Windows EC2 instances to enable Systems Manager to manage and deploy software packages is incorrect because while AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore is indeed required for EC2 instances to be managed by Systems Manager, this is a foundational prerequisite that should already be in place, not an action that fulfills the requirement of deploying custom software with minimal administrative effort. The question primarily asks for the steps to automate software deployment, not to configure basic SSM connectivity.

questions.question:
A multinational company has a complex architecture that spans multiple AWS regions and accounts. Recently, there has been an initiative to optimize the cost and performance of its AWS resources, especially focusing on AWS Lambda functions. The aim is to identify and implement optimal resource configurations for each Lambda function of the company’s serverless apps.

Which of the following recommendations should the CloudOps Engineer provide?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service provides cost and performance optimization recommendations specifically for Lambda functions?
choices: Use AWS Trusted Advisor for general best practice recommendations, including those for AWS Lambda.
Use AWS Serverless Application Repository to deploy optimized serverless applications.
Use AWS CloudTrail Insights to detect unusual activity and adjust Lambda functions accordingly.
Enable AWS Compute Optimizer to receive specific performance and cost recommendations for Lambda functions.
correctAnswers: Enable AWS Compute Optimizer to receive specific performance and cost recommendations for Lambda functions.
questions.explanation:
AWS Compute Optimizer is a service that analyzes the configuration and utilization metrics of your AWS resources. It reports whether your resources are optimal and generates optimization recommendations to reduce the cost and improve the performance of your workloads.

Compute Optimizer also provides graphs showing recent utilization metric history data, as well as projected utilization for recommendations, which you can use to evaluate which recommendation provides the best price-performance trade-off. The analysis and visualization of your usage patterns can help you decide when to move or resize your running resources, and still meet your performance and capacity requirements.

Compute Optimizer helps you optimize two categories of Lambda functions:

– The first category includes Lambda functions that may be overprovisioned in memory sizes. You may consider downsizing the memory sizes of these functions to save costs.

– The second category includes compute-intensive Lambda functions that may benefit from additional CPU power.

You may consider increasing their memory sizes to trigger an equivalent increase in CPU available to these functions and reduce runtime. For functions that do not fall under any of these categories, Compute Optimizer does not deliver recommendations for them.

Hence, the correct answer is: Enable AWS Compute Optimizer to receive specific performance and cost recommendations for Lambda functions.

The option that says: Use AWS Trusted Advisor for general best practice recommendations, including those for AWS Lambda is incorrect. Trusted Advisor offers broader best practice recommendations, which can include potential Lambda optimizations. However, its insights are more general and not as finely tuned for Lambda optimization as those from Compute Optimizer.

The option that says: Use AWS CloudTrail Insights to detect unusual activity and adjust Lambda functions accordingly is incorrect. While AWS CloudTrail Insights can detect anomalies in Lambda invocations, it doesn’t give guidance on optimizing configurations. Its primary purpose is enhancing security and aiding in troubleshooting operational issues, not in the detailed performance or cost optimization of Lambda resources.

The option that says: Use AWS Serverless Application Repository to deploy optimized serverless applications is incorrect. The repository doesn’t analyze or provide recommendations for optimizing existing Lambda functions. It is primarily about streamlining the deployment of new serverless applications.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer is troubleshooting performance issues on an Amazon EC2 instance that hosts a high-throughput database. The instance is attached to multiple Amazon Elastic Block Store (Amazon EBS) volumes. To monitor I/O activity, the administrator sets up Amazon CloudWatch alarms using the DiskReadBytes and DiskWriteBytes metrics.

A custom script deployed on the EC2 instance detects that the EBS volume metrics have gone beyond the configured thresholds. Yet, the associated CloudWatch alarms stay in the OK state and do not switch to ALARM, even though the script verifies that the limits were breached.

What solution should be implemented to ensure that the alarms for volume I/O metrics work as expected and trigger alerts during incidents?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which CloudWatch metrics correctly track EBS volume-level I/O activity instead of instance-level disk metrics?
choices: Ensure that the AWS Systems Manager Agent (SSM Agent) is running on the EC2 instance to allow CloudWatch to collect EBS metrics.
Attach the EBS volumes to a different EC2 instance type that supports higher IOPS to ensure alarms trigger.
Enable detailed monitoring on the EC2 instance to ensure EBS metrics are published to CloudWatch.
Modify the existing CloudWatch Alarms to use the VolumeReadBytes and VolumeWriteBytes metrics to monitor the EBS volumes.
correctAnswers: Modify the existing CloudWatch Alarms to use the VolumeReadBytes and VolumeWriteBytes metrics to monitor the EBS volumes.
questions.explanation:
You can set up alarms to monitor metrics and send notifications or automatically make changes to the resources you are tracking when a threshold is exceeded. For instance, you can observe the CPU usage and disk reads and writes of your Amazon EC2 instances. Based on this data, you can decide whether to launch additional instances to accommodate increased load, or you can terminate underused instances to save money.

In Amazon CloudWatch, you have the option to create both metric alarms and composite alarms.

– A metric alarm watches a single CloudWatch metric or the result of a math expression based on CloudWatch metrics. The alarm performs one or more actions based on the value of the metric or expression relative to a threshold over a number of time periods. The action can be sending a notification to an Amazon SNS topic, performing an Amazon EC2 action or an Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling action, starting an investigation in CloudWatch investigations operational investigations, or creating an OpsItem or incident in Systems Manager.

– A composite alarm includes a rule expression that takes into account the alarm states of other alarms that you have created. The composite alarm goes into ALARM state only if all conditions of the rule are met. The alarms specified in a composite alarm’s rule expression can include metric alarms and other composite alarms. Using composite alarms can reduce alarm noise. You can create multiple metric alarms, and also create a composite alarm and set up alerts only for the composite alarm. For example, a composite might go into ALARM state only when all of the underlying metric alarms are in ALARM state.

A metric alarm can exist in the following possible states:

– OK – The metric or expression is within the defined threshold.

– ALARM – The metric or expression is outside of the defined threshold.

– INSUFFICIENT_DATA – The alarm has just started, the metric is not available, or not enough data is available for the metric to determine the alarm state.

VolumeReadBytes and VolumeWriteBytes metrics

All Amazon EBS volume types automatically send 1-minute metrics to CloudWatch, but only when the volume is attached to an instance.

VolumeReadBytes provides information on the read operations in a specified period of time.

VolumeWriteBytes provides information on the write operations in a specified period of time.

Hence, the correct answer is: Modify the existing CloudWatch Alarms to use the VolumeReadBytes and VolumeWriteBytes metrics to monitor the EBS volumes. These are the specific CloudWatch metrics that track I/O activity at the EBS volume level. Unlike instance-level metrics, they reflect actual read/write throughput for each volume, ensuring alarms trigger accurately when thresholds are breached.

The option that says: Attach the EBS volumes to a different EC2 instance type that supports higher IOPS to ensure alarms trigger is incorrect because changing the instance type only improves performance, but does not affect how CloudWatch alarms are triggered. The issue lies in the metric selection, not the instance’s IOPS capability.

The option that says: Enable detailed monitoring on the EC2 instance to ensure EBS metrics are published to CloudWatch is incorrect because detailed monitoring typically enhances EC2 instance metrics, not EBS volume metrics. EBS metrics are published to CloudWatch independently and do not require detailed EC2 monitoring.

The option that says: Ensure that the AWS Systems Manager Agent (SSM Agent) is running on the EC2 instance to allow CloudWatch to collect EBS metrics is incorrect because the SSM Agent is used for Systems Manager tasks like automation and patching. It does not influence CloudWatch’s ability to collect EBS metrics, which are published automatically by AWS.

questions.question:
A company operates a business-critical application hosted on Amazon EC2 instances, orchestrated behind an Application Load Balancer (ALB). The EC2 instances are managed by an Auto Scaling group.
