Recently, users have reported intermittent sluggishness and unresponsiveness in the application. From the initial investigation, some of the instances are experiencing high CPU load. The CloudOps Engineer intends to create an Amazon CloudWatch dashboard that provides an overview of CPU metrics for all EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group. The dashboard must include metrics from newly launched EC2 instances as part of the Auto Scaling group.

What is the MOST operationally efficient way that should be implemented to satisfy the requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you create a dynamic CloudWatch dashboard that automatically includes CPU metrics for all Auto Scaling group instances?
choices: Utilize CloudWatch Metrics Explorer to create a visualization. Configure the visualization for the CPUUtilization metric and filter based on the aws:autoscaling:groupName tag. Add the visualization as a widget to a CloudWatch dashboard.

Utilize the EC2 Instance-launch Lifecycle Action event from the Amazon EventBridge to invoke an AWS Lambda function. The Lambda function will update a CloudWatch dashboard to monitor the CPUUtilization metric of the newly launched instance.

Utilize the autoscaling:EC2_INSTANCE_LAUNCH notification from the Auto Scaling group to invoke an Amazon SNS Topic. The SNS topic will call an AWS Lambda function, which updates the CloudWatch dashboard to monitor the CPUUtilization metric of the newly launched instance.

Utilize CloudWatch Metrics Explorer to create a visualization. Create a custom tag in the Auto Scaling Group. Configure the visualization for the CPUUtilization metric and filter based on the custom ASG tag. Add the visualization as a widget to a CloudWatch dashboard.
correctAnswers: Utilize CloudWatch Metrics Explorer to create a visualization. Configure the visualization for the CPUUtilization metric and filter based on the aws:autoscaling:groupName tag. Add the visualization as a widget to a CloudWatch dashboard.
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudWatch monitors your Amazon Web Services (AWS) resources and the applications you run on AWS in real time. You can use CloudWatch to collect and track metrics, which are variables you can measure for your resources and applications. You can also create customized views of the metrics and alarms for your AWS resources by using CloudWatch dashboards.

You can customize the dashboard with the metrics from your resources filtered based on tags. Metrics explorer is a tag-based tool that enables you to filter, aggregate, and visualize your metrics by tags and resource properties, to enhance observability for your services. This gives you a flexible and dynamic troubleshooting experience, so that you to create multiple graphs at a time and use these graphs to build your application health dashboards.

Amazon CloudWatch

Metrics explorer visualizations are dynamic, so if a matching resource is created after you create a metrics explorer widget and add it to a CloudWatch dashboard, the new resource automatically appears in the explorer widget.

The Auto Scaling group automatically adds a tag to instances with a key of aws:autoscaling:groupName and a value of the Auto Scaling group name.

You can use the Metrics Explorer to create a CloudWatch dashboard that provides an overview of CPU metrics for all EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group including the newly launched EC2 instances of the Auto Scaling Group based on tag aws:autoscaling:groupName.

Hence, the correct answer is: Utilize CloudWatch Metrics Explorer to create a visualization. Configure the visualization for the CPUUtilization metric and filter based on the aws:autoscaling:groupName tag. Add the visualization as a widget to a CloudWatch dashboard.

The option that says: Utilize the autoscaling:EC2_INSTANCE_LAUNCH notification from the Auto Scaling group to invoke an Amazon SNS Topic. The SNS topic will call an AWS Lambda function, which updates the CloudWatch dashboard to monitor the CPUUtilization metric of the newly launched instance is incorrect. While this solution can automatically update the CloudWatch dashboard every time a new EC2 instance is launched, it is still inadequate because it entails significant operational overhead. The CloudOps Engineer must create an SNS topic and maintain custom code in the Lambda function, and creating and maintaining the code in Lambda will require significant effort. The CloudOps Engineer should primarily use the CloudWatch Metrics Explore solution instead.

The option that says: Utilize CloudWatch Metrics Explorer to create a visualization. Create a custom tag in the Auto Scaling Group. Configure the visualization for the CPUUtilization metric and filter based on the custom ASG tag. Add the visualization as a widget to a CloudWatch dashboard is incorrect. Even though it’s true that this will provide an overview of CPU metrics for all EC2 instances in the Auto Scaling group, it is still an inferior solution because of operational efficiency. The added step of creating a custom tag and utilizing the Metric Explorer to filter based on the custom tag is unnecessary. The ASG will already tag all the instances under it with aws:autoscaling:groupName the metric, including the newly launched instances. Hence, the CloudOps Engineer can use this tag instead.

The option that says: Utilize the EC2 Instance-launch Lifecycle Action event from the Amazon EventBridge to invoke an AWS Lambda function. The Lambda function will update a CloudWatch dashboard to monitor the CPUUtilization metric of the newly launched instance is incorrect. Although utilizing Amazon EventBridge to invoke a Lambda function to update the CloudWatch dashboard can definitely meet the requirements, it typically takes effort to create and maintain a custom Lambda code and set up an Amazon EventBridge rule. This solution is not the most operationally efficient way to meet the requirement.

questions.question:
A financial services firm operating under a multi-account AWS Organizations structure is preparing for an upcoming regulatory audit. The compliance team must ensure that all AWS accounts are continuously evaluated against the Center for Internet Security (CIS) AWS Foundations Benchmark, including any accounts added in the future.

Which approach meets this requirement while minimizing ongoing manual effort?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service continuously evaluates all organization accounts against the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark automatically?
choices: Designate a delegated Amazon GuardDuty administrator account, enable auto-activation for member accounts, and configure it to run CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark scans.
Use AWS Audit Manager with the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark framework to continuously assess all accounts across the organization, including future member accounts.
Deploy an AWS Lambda function that automatically enrolls each new account into AWS Security Hub and enables the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark standard upon account creation.
Designate an AWS Security Hub delegated administrator account, enable automatic member enrollment, and activate the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark standard.
correctAnswers: Designate an AWS Security Hub delegated administrator account, enable automatic member enrollment, and activate the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark standard.
questions.explanation:
AWS Security Hub is a cloud security posture management service that continuously evaluates AWS account configurations against security standards, including the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark. When integrated with AWS Organizations, Security Hub allows a delegated administrator account to manage all member accounts centrally. Enabling automatic enrollment ensures that every account, including those added in the future, is immediately brought under scope without any manual intervention.

The setup requires three steps: designating a Security Hub delegated administrator through the Organizations management account, enabling auto-enrollment for new accounts, and activating the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark standard. Once configured, Security Hub continuously runs benchmark checks across all enrolled accounts and surfaces findings in a centralized dashboard with no scripts or repeated per-account setup required.

Screenshot of the AWS Security Hub on the AWS Console.

Security Hub supports multiple compliance standards that can be enabled across all accounts simultaneously:

– CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark: Evaluates account-level settings such as IAM policies, CloudTrail configuration, and VPC flow logging.

– AWS Foundational Security Best Practices: A broader set of AWS-curated controls spanning services like S3, RDS, and EC2.

– PCI DSS: Controls aligned to payment card industry security requirements.

Any approach that relies on custom functions or manual enrollment introduces overhead and coverage gaps that the native Organizations integration eliminates.

Hence, the correct answer is: Designate an AWS Security Hub delegated administrator account, enable automatic member enrollment, and activate the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark standard.

The option that says: Use AWS Audit Manager with the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark framework to continuously assess all accounts across the organization, including future member accounts is incorrect. AWS Audit Manager does support the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark framework and integrates with AWS Organizations, but it is designed primarily for audit evidence collection and reporting — not continuous, real-time compliance monitoring. It periodically gathers evidence to support audits rather than performing ongoing automated control evaluations, which is what the scenario requires.

The option that says: Deploy an AWS Lambda function that automatically enrolls each new account into AWS Security Hub and enables the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark standard upon account creation is incorrect. This option points to the correct service, but replaces a native built-in feature with an unnecessary custom automation layer. Security Hub already handles automatic member enrollment natively through its Organizations integration — adding a Lambda function simply overcomplicates something that already works out of the box.

The option that says: Designate a delegated Amazon GuardDuty administrator account, enable auto-activation for member accounts, and configure it to run CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark scans is incorrect. GuardDuty is a threat detection service that monitors for malicious activity using log and network data. It supports Organizations-level delegation, but it does not support compliance standard assessments like the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark. That capability belongs solely to Security Hub, as GuardDuty and Security Hub serve two very different purposes.

questions.question:
A healthcare technology company recently completed a HIPAA compliance audit that flagged the absence of DNS-level threat controls across AWS-hosted patient data systems. The compliance team must implement a solution that intercepts and blocks malicious domain lookups before queries reach application workloads.

Which approach fulfills this requirement while operating natively within the AWS DNS resolution path?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service natively intercepts and blocks malicious DNS queries within a VPC's DNS resolution path?
choices: Deploy AWS Network Firewall with a stateful domain list rule group to filter outbound DNS traffic at the subnet boundary.
Set up AWS Config rules to evaluate DNS query logs for anomalies and use an AWS Lambda function to add offending domains to a block list enforced by a network ACL.
Enable Amazon GuardDuty with DNS log analysis to detect malicious domain activity and invoke automated blocking through AWS Lambda.
Associate an Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rule group with the VPC to intercept and block queries for known malicious domains.
correctAnswers: Associate an Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rule group with the VPC to intercept and block queries for known malicious domains.
questions.explanation:
Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall is a managed service that filters and controls DNS queries originating from within a VPC. By creating a rule group that references domain lists, and then associating that rule group directly with the target VPC, all DNS queries flowing through Route 53 Resolver are evaluated before a response is returned. When a query matches a domain on a block list, the firewall returns a configured response such as NODATA or NXDOMAIN, preventing the workload from ever establishing a connection to a malicious endpoint. This interception happens natively in the DNS resolution path, requiring no additional network appliances.

A DNS Firewall rule group contains one or more rules, each mapping an action (BLOCK, ALLOW, or ALERT) to a domain list. AWS-managed domain lists are available for common threat categories, and custom domain lists can supplement them. The rule group is associated with a VPC using the associate-firewall-rule-group API action or through the Route 53 console. Once associated, every query resolved through the VPC’s default resolver is subject to evaluation without any routing changes or additional infrastructure.

AWS Console showing Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rule group associated with a VPC, including a BLOCK action applied to a malicious domain list

DNS Firewall supports two primary rule actions:

– BLOCK: Returns a configurable failure response (NODATA, NXDOMAIN, or OVERRIDE with a custom CNAME) to the querying resource, preventing resolution entirely.

– ALERT: Logs the matching query to CloudWatch Logs without blocking it, which is useful during a dry-run period before enforcing blocks.

DNS Firewall does not apply to DNS traffic that bypasses Route 53 Resolver, such as queries sent directly to a third-party DNS resolver over a custom port.

Hence, the correct answer is: Associate an Amazon Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall rule group with the VPC to intercept and block queries for known malicious domains.

The option that says: Enable Amazon GuardDuty with DNS log analysis to detect malicious domain activity and invoke automated blocking through AWS Lambda is incorrect. GuardDuty primarily detects threats by analyzing DNS logs, VPC flow logs, and CloudTrail events, but it does not natively intercept or block DNS queries in real time. Triggering a Lambda function in response to a GuardDuty finding introduces remediation latency, meaning the malicious query has already resolved and the connection may already be established before any blocking action takes effect.

The option that says: Deploy AWS Network Firewall with a stateful domain list rule group to filter outbound DNS traffic at the subnet boundary is incorrect. Network Firewall can typically inspect and filter outbound DNS traffic at the network layer using stateful domain list rules, but it operates as a separate inline appliance that requires traffic to be routed through a dedicated firewall subnet. This approach adds infrastructure complexity and does not place the control natively within the DNS resolution path the way Route 53 Resolver DNS Firewall does.

The option that says: Set up AWS Config rules to evaluate DNS query logs for anomalies and use an AWS Lambda function to add offending domains to a block list enforced by a network ACL is incorrect. AWS Config is a configuration compliance service, not a real-time DNS filtering tool. Config rules evaluate resource configuration states on a periodic or change-triggered basis, so this approach simply cannot intercept a DNS query at resolution time. Network ACLs also operate at the IP layer and cannot match or block traffic based on domain names.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer is deploying its multitier enterprise application using AWS CloudFormation StackSets to two distinct AWS Regions within a single AWS account. During the orchestration process, a stack operation encounters failure in one of the Regions, resulting in the deployment status being flagged as OUTDATED.

Which of the following is the MOST likely root cause of this particular failure?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is the most likely cause of an OUTDATED status when deploying a CloudFormation StackSet across regions?
choices: The CloudFormation template is attempting to create global resources that are not unique, such as S3 buckets.
Modifications were made to the template on the local disk and CloudFormation registry, but it hasn't been submitted to CloudFormation for execution.
An outdated version of the CloudFormation API or AWS CDK was used by the SysOps administrator.
Deployment of the stack in the Region remains pending or incomplete.
correctAnswers: The CloudFormation template is attempting to create global resources that are not unique, such as S3 buckets.
questions.explanation:
AWS CloudFormation is a powerful tool for automating the deployment of infrastructure resources in a repeatable and predictable manner. With CloudFormation, users can create templates that describe the infrastructure resources they need, such as virtual machines, security groups, and load balancers, and then use those templates to create and manage their resources—defining infrastructure as code becomes version-controlled, tested, and reusable, simplifying infrastructure management.

AWS CloudFormation StackSets is an added feature of CloudFormation that enables users to deploy infrastructure resources across multiple accounts and regions with a single template. With StackSets, users can define a template once and simultaneously deploy it to multiple accounts and regions. This is useful for organizations managing infrastructure resources across different regions and accounts, ensuring consistency and compliance with company standards. StackSets also provides centralized control over updates and rollbacks, simplifying the management of large-scale deployments.

There can be several common causes for stack operation failure. For example, in the event that the stack operation failed and the stack instance status is OUTDATED, the following are the possible culprits:

– Insufficient permissions in a target account for creating resources that are specified in your template.

– The AWS CloudFormation template might have errors. Validate the template in AWS CloudFormation and fix errors before trying to create your stack set.

– The template could be trying to create global resources that must be unique but aren’t, such as S3 buckets.

– A specified target account number doesn’t exist. Check the target account numbers that you specified on the Set Deployment Options page of the wizard.

– The administrator account does not have a trust relationship with the target account.

– The maximum number of a resource that is specified in your template already exists in your target account. For example, you might have reached the limit of allowed IAM roles in a target account, but the template creates more IAM roles.

– You have reached the maximum number of stacks that are allowed in a stack set.

In the given scenario, the deployment status was flagged as "OUTDATED" due to a deployment conflict caused by the CloudFormation template aiming to create a non-unique global resource. This indicates that the template was attempting to create a resource that already existed, failing in one of the Regions. Ensuring that the CloudFormation template defines unique resource names or IDs is essential to prevent conflicts during resource creation.

Hence, the correct answer is: The CloudFormation template is attempting to create global resources that are not unique, such as S3 buckets.

The option that says: Modifications were made to the template on the local disk and CloudFormation registry, but it hasn’t been submitted to CloudFormation for execution is incorrect. This option is unlikely to cause the "OUTDATED" status. If changes were made to the template on the local disk but not submitted, it would not result in the "OUTDATED" status of the stack operation. Instead, the changes wouldn’t be applied until the updated template is submitted. In addition, the CloudFormation registry just lets you manage public and private extensions such as resources, modules, and hooks that are available for use in your AWS account. It’s quite unlikely that the CloudFormation registry will cause this issue.

The option that says: Deployment of the stack in the Region remains pending or incomplete is incorrect. While a pending or incomplete deployment could cause issues, it would not directly lead to "OUTDATED" status. If the deployment was pending or incomplete, the status would likely be something like "IN_PROGRESS" or "FAILED" but not "OUTDATED".

The option that says: An outdated version of the CloudFormation API or AWS CDK was used by the SysOps administrator is incorrect. Using an outdated version of the CloudFormation API might cause compatibility issues or errors, but it wouldn’t directly result in "OUTDATED" status of the stack operation. API version mismatches typically lead to errors or unexpected behavior, not precisely the"OUTDATED" status.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer oversees the Amazon S3 buckets used by a video-sharing application for a company. The application allows users to upload and share videos of various sizes and resolutions. The CloudOps Engineer notices that there are 7 GB of incomplete multipart uploads present in one of the S3 buckets within the company’s AWS account. The CloudOps Engineer needs to address and minimize the number of incomplete multipart upload objects in the S3 bucket.

Which solution will satisfy the requirement with the LEAST operational overhead?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is the least-overhead way to automatically clean up incomplete multipart uploads in an S3 bucket?
choices: Configure a scheduled EventBridge rule and a Lambda function to delete incomplete multipart uploads daily.
Update the application code to upload files to the S3 bucket using S3 TransferUtility.
Configure an S3 Object Lambda Access Point for PUT API operation.
Configure an S3 Lifecycle rule in the S3 bucket to automatically remove expired object delete markers or incomplete multipart uploads.
correctAnswers: Configure an S3 Lifecycle rule in the S3 bucket to automatically remove expired object delete markers or incomplete multipart uploads.
questions.explanation:
Multipart upload allows you to upload a single object as a set of parts. Each part is a contiguous portion of the object’s data. You can upload these object parts independently and in any order. If transmission of any part fails, you can retransmit that part without affecting other parts. After all parts of your object are uploaded, Amazon S3 assembles these parts and creates the object. In general, when your object size reaches 100 MB, you should consider using multipart uploads instead of uploading the object in a single operation.

Multipart upload is a three-step process: You initiate the upload, you upload the object parts, and after you have uploaded all the parts, you complete the multipart upload. Upon receiving the complete multipart upload request, Amazon S3 constructs the object from the uploaded parts, and you can then access the object just as you would any other object in your bucket.

However, there are instances in which multipart uploads are not successful. Upon receiving the complete multipart upload request, Amazon S3 assembles the parts and creates an object. If you don’t send the complete multipart upload request successfully, Amazon S3 does not assemble the parts and does not create any object. You are billed for all storage associated with uploaded parts. This is why it is important to stop and delete an incomplete multipart upload.

You can utilize bucket lifecycle configuration to do this. Amazon S3 supports a bucket lifecycle rule that you can use to direct Amazon S3 to stop multipart uploads that aren’t completed within a specified number of days after being initiated. When a multipart upload isn’t completed within the specified time frame, it becomes eligible for an abort operation. Amazon S3 then stops the multipart upload and deletes the parts associated with the multipart upload.

To automatically manage incomplete multipart uploads, you can use the S3 console to create a lifecycle rule to expire incomplete multipart upload bytes from your bucket after a specified number of days.

In the scope of the Lifecycle rule make sure to choose “This rule applies to all objects in the bucket”, and choose “I acknowledge that this rule applies to all objects in the bucket”. This will create a lifecycle rule for all objects in the bucket.

Under Lifecycle rule actions, select “Delete expired object delete markers or incomplete multipart uploads” and select “Delete incomplete multipart uploads”. This will delete all the incomplete multipart uploads upon expiration of the set days.

Hence, the correct answer is: Configure an S3 Lifecycle rule in the S3 bucket to automatically remove expired object delete markers or incomplete multipart uploads.

The option that says: Configure a scheduled EventBridge rule and a Lambda function to delete incomplete multipart uploads daily is incorrect. Although this solution can resolve the issue, it requires significant operational overhead; thus, this implementation isn’t sufficient. The SysOps administrator must build a custom Lambda function code to list the incomplete multipart uploads and delete them to implement this. Creating a custom code would be too much, considering the administrator can resolve the issue using a bucket configuration lifecycle rule.

The option that says: Configure an S3 Object Lambda Access Point for PUT API operation is incorrect. This solution cannot be used for this scenario as the S3 Object Lambda Access Point does not support PUT API operation. The S3 Object Lambda Access Point is primarily used to customize the data requested from the S3 during GET, LIST, and HEAD requests. It allows the applications to customize the requested data by using AWS Lambda. It can redact confidential information, resize images dynamically, modify and improve your objects’ metadata, etc. before sending it back to the applications. Hence, this solution is not suitable for the scenario.

The option that says: Update the application code to upload files to the S3 bucket using S3 TransferUtility is incorrect. While this solution can improve the upload and download of objects for the application, it is still inadequate as it does not directly handle the deletion of incomplete multipart uploads. It is primarily designed for efficiently uploading and downloading files, especially large files, by automatically using multipart uploads in the background. However, it doesn’t handle the cleanup or management of incomplete multipart uploads. The SysOps administrator should use the S3 Bucket Lifecycle Configuration instead.

questions.question:
A web application is hosted on 4 On-Demand Amazon EC2 instances, which are deployed evenly across two Availability Zones behind an Application Load Balancer. Monitoring through Amazon CloudWatch indicates that one of the EC2 instances is operating at approximately 95% CPU utilization, while the other three instances only have 10% CPU utilization.

Which of the following is the most suitable fix for this issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you fix uneven load distribution where one ALB instance has 95% CPU while others have 10%?
choices: Set an expiry on the cookie created by the application that is prior to the current date and time.
Disable connection draining in the Application Load Balancer.
Replace your Application Load Balancer with a Network Load Balancer.
Disable Cross-zone load balancing in the Application Load Balancer.
correctAnswers: Set an expiry on the cookie created by the application that is prior to the current date and time.
questions.explanation:
By default, an Application Load Balancer routes each request independently to a registered target based on the chosen load-balancing algorithm. However, you can use the sticky session feature (also known as session affinity) to enable the load balancer to bind a user’s session to a specific target. This ensures that all requests from the user during the session are sent to the same target. This feature is useful for servers that maintain state information in order to provide a continuous experience to clients. To use sticky sessions, the client must support cookies.

Application Load Balancer - Load balancer generated cookie

When scaling up, if the number of targets increases considerably, there is potential for unequal distribution of load due to stickiness. In this scenario, you can rebalance the load on your targets using the following two options:

– Set an expiry on the cookie generated by the application that is prior to the current date and time. This will prevent clients from sending the cookie to the Application Load Balancer, which will restart the process of establishing stickiness.

– Set a very short duration on the load balancer’s application-based stickiness configuration, for example, 1 second. This forces the Application Load Balancer to reestablish stickiness even if the cookie set by the target has not expired.

Hence, the correct answer is: Set an expiry on the cookie created by the application that is prior to the current date and time.

The option that says: Disable Cross-zone load balancing in the Application Load Balancer is incorrect because cross-zone load balancing just sets each load balancer node of your Application Load Balancer to distribute requests evenly across the registered instances in all enabled Availability Zones. The issue is the high CPU Utilization of one of the EC2 instances because it is bound to a user’s session, which can be solved by rebalancing the load on your targets.

The option that says: Disable connection draining in the Application Load Balancer is incorrect because the connection draining feature simply enables the load balancer to complete in-flight requests made to instances that are de-registering or unhealthy.

The option that says: Replace your Application Load Balancer with a Network Load Balancer is incorrect because this solution entails an architecture change on your part and may take a lot of time. It is still better to rebalance the load on your ALB to immediately fix the issue.

questions.question:
The company is currently operating a mission-critical application on a fleet of Amazon EC2 instances. To enhance cost efficiency, the company is planning to migrate its application from using EC2 instances to AWS Fargate as the compute engine. The application will be containerized and hosted on Amazon EKS which will run the Kubernetes Pods on AWS Fargate.

The CloudOps Engineer must select a suitable purchasing option to minimize its Fargate costs. In addition, the Engineer must maximize the available discounts in AWS and ensure that there are no unused reservations.

Which of the following can be done to meet the above requirements in the MOST cost-effective way?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which Savings Plan and payment option provides the maximum discount for AWS Fargate workloads?
choices: Choose one-year Compute Savings Plans with a No Upfront purchasing option.
Choose one-year EC2 Instance Savings Plans with All Upfront purchasing option.
Choose one-year Compute Savings Plans with a Partial Upfront purchasing option.
Choose one-year Compute Savings Plans with All Upfront purchasing option.
correctAnswers: Choose one-year Compute Savings Plans with All Upfront purchasing option.
questions.explanation:
Savings Plans is another flexible pricing model that provides savings of up to 72% on your AWS compute usage. This pricing model offers lower prices on Amazon EC2 instances usage, regardless of instance family, size, OS, tenancy, or AWS Region, and also applies to AWS Fargate and AWS Lambda usage.

Savings Plans offer significant savings over On-Demand Instances, just like EC2 Reserved Instances, in exchange for a commitment to use a specific amount of compute power (measured in $/hour) for a one or three-year period. You can sign up for Savings Plans for a one- or three-year term and easily manage your plans by taking advantage of recommendations, performance reporting, and budget alerts in the AWS Cost Explorer.

AWS offers two types of Savings Plans:

Compute Savings Plans provide the most flexibility and help to reduce your costs by up to 66% (just like Convertible RIs). These plans automatically apply to EC2 instance usage regardless of instance family, size, AZ, Region, operating system, or tenancy, and also apply to Fargate and Lambda usage. For example, with Compute Savings Plans, you can change from C4 to M5 instances, shift a workload from EU (Ireland) to Europe (London), or move a workload from Amazon EC2 to Fargate or Lambda at any time and automatically continue to pay the Savings Plans price.

EC2 Instance Savings Plans provide the lowest prices, offering savings up to 72% (just like Standard RIs) in exchange for a commitment to the usage of individual instance families in a Region (for example, M5 usage in N. Virginia). This automatically reduces your cost on the selected instance family in that region regardless of AZ, size, operating system, or tenancy. EC2 Instance Savings Plans give you the flexibility to change your usage between instances within a family in that Region. For example, you can move from c5.xlarge running Windows to c5.2xlarge running Linux and automatically benefit from the Savings Plans prices.

By using Compute Savings Plans, you’ll enjoy automatic price reductions when transitioning from C4 to C6g instances, transferring workloads from EU (Ireland) to EU (London), or migrating workloads from Amazon EC2 to AWS Fargate or AWS Lambda.

The payment options for the Savings Plans can be:

– No Upfront

– Partial Upfront

– All Upfront

Take note that in reserving your computing capacity, you’ll gain the most discount if you paid everything upfront. This means that the cheapest option among the payment options above is the All Upfront type, and conversely, the most expensive one is the No Upfront.

Hence, the correct answer is the option that says: Choose one-year Compute Savings Plans with All Upfront purchasing option.

The option that says: Choose one-year Compute Savings Plans with a Partial Upfront purchasing option is incorrect. Although it is more cost-effective than the No Upfront option, its cost is still higher compared with the All Upfront option. You will be paying a partial amount of your computing reservation on your first month, which will grant you some savings compared with the No Upfront option.

The option that says: Choose one-year Compute Savings Plans with a No Upfront purchasing option is incorrect. Among all of the one-year Compute Savings types, this one has the highest cost since you are basically paying this on a monthly basis without any much discount at all. Remember that in computing capacity reservation, you’ll be able to avail the highest discount if you pay upfront and not the other way around.

The option that says: Choose one-year EC2 Instance Savings Plans with All Upfront purchasing option is incorrect. Although an Instance Savings Plans type also provides you with a significant discount compared to On-Demand instance pricing, it’s important to note that it does not automatically reduce costs when transitioning a workload from Amazon EC2 to AWS Fargate or AWS Lambda. You have to use the Compute Savings Plans type instead.

questions.question: A mobile development company has various AWS resources to support its various mobile products. To keep control of costs, they have requested for you to get the billing alerts for your AWS account once it reaches a certain limit. Which of the following should you enable before you can receive billing alerts in AWS?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What must you enable first in AWS before you can create billing alerts on your account?
choices: Enable billing alerts in Account Preferences of the AWS Console.
Request AWS support to notify you on estimated charges.
Request an AWS support partner to notify you on estimated charges
Enable billing alerts in CloudWatch Console.
correctAnswers: Enable billing alerts in Account Preferences of the AWS Console.
questions.explanation:
Enabling billing alerts in Account Preferences of the AWS Console is the correct answer. Before you can create an alarm for your estimated charges, you must enable billing alerts on your Accounts Preferences page first, so that you can monitor your estimated AWS charges and create an alarm using billing metric data. After you enable billing alerts, you cannot disable data collection, but you can delete any billing alarms that you created.

The below snapshot from the AWS Console shows where this can be enabled:

Enabling billing alerts in CloudWatch Console is incorrect because you need to enable billing alerts on your Preferences page.

Requesting AWS support to notify you on estimated charges and requesting an AWS support partner to notify you on estimated charges are both incorrect because AWS can already provide you with the options to enable and configure billing alerts for your account.

questions.question:
An e-commerce company operates its web applications on a Linux-based Amazon EC2 instance. The operations engineer is tasked with ensuring that the process running the application is automatically restarted in case of unexpected downtime. The engineer has already configured an Amazon CloudWatch agent across the instances for monitoring purposes.

What should the engineer do next to fulfill the requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you automatically restart an application process on EC2 when it fails using CloudWatch and Systems Manager?
choices: Modify the CloudWatch agent file configuration and include the procstat plugin. Use the append-config option without overwriting existing settings. Leverage Amazon EventBridge to create a rule that detects the application’s process failure and automatically triggers an AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Automation runbook to restart the application when unexpected downtime occurs.

Set up a StatsD configuration in the CloudWatch agent to collect detailed operational metrics of the application. Establish an Amazon EventBridge rule that executes an AWS SSM Automation document to reboot the application upon failure.

Configure the CloudWatch agent with a StatsD monitoring setup specific to the application. Set up a CloudWatch alarm that triggers an AWS SSM Automation document to reboot the application in case of failure.

Configure the CloudWatch agent to monitor the application’s process using procstat monitoring. Set up a CloudWatch alarm to trigger an AWS Systems Manager Automation document for immediate EC2 instance restart upon application failure.
correctAnswers: Modify the CloudWatch agent file configuration and include the procstat plugin. Use the append-config option without overwriting existing settings. Leverage Amazon EventBridge to create a rule that detects the application’s process failure and automatically triggers an AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Automation runbook to restart the application when unexpected downtime occurs.
questions.explanation:
Amazon EventBridge is a service that allows you to connect applications with data from AWS services, your applications, and SaaS applications. It helps you build responsive, event-driven architectures that can scale up and be loosely coupled. The service manages the data flow between software components and services, making it easier to create scalable architectures. EventBridge can define events in JSON objects, triggering targets such as AWS Lambda functions, Amazon SNS topics, or AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Automation documents. This feature makes it an essential tool for automating responses to operational changes or system states, such as application failures or resource state changes. It improves the resilience and responsiveness of cloud applications.

AWS Systems Manager Automation is a part of AWS Systems Manager, which provides a framework for automating administrative tasks across AWS resources. With SSM Automation, you can create runbooks that define a sequence of operations for tasks such as patching operating systems, updating drivers, or restarting applications or services when they become unresponsive. You can trigger this automation manually or automatically in response to specific events, such as alerts from Amazon CloudWatch or state changes detected by Amazon EventBridge. This integration capability with other AWS services, like EventBridge, enables the orchestration of complex operational workflows that can respond dynamically to the state of the application and infrastructure. This ensures high availability and reduces the need for manual intervention in routine or recovery tasks.

Amazon EventBridge - Event pattern

Amazon EventBridge and AWS Systems Manager Automation create and manage event-driven automation solutions. With EventBridge, application state changes can be detected and routed, while SSM Automation can execute recovery or maintenance tasks. This combination improves operational efficiency and enhances the reliability and performance of the services.

Hence, the correct answer is: Modify the CloudWatch agent file configuration and include the procstat plugin. Use the append-config option without overwriting existing settings. Leverage Amazon EventBridge to create a rule that detects the application’s process failure and automatically triggers an AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Automation runbook to restart the application when unexpected downtime occurs.

The option that says: Configure the CloudWatch agent with a StatsD monitoring setup specific to the application. Set up a CloudWatch alarm that triggers an AWS SSM Automation document to reboot the application in case of failure is incorrect. Although StatsD is a useful tool for collecting metrics, it is primarily designed for aggregating statistical data like counters and timers, rather than monitoring specific application states. This option lacks specificity in monitoring for application states, which is critical in this scenario.

The option that says: Set up a StatsD configuration in the CloudWatch agent to collect detailed operational metrics of the application. Establish an Amazon EventBridge rule that executes an AWS SSM Automation document to reboot the application upon failure is incorrect. When creating a rule in Amazon EventBridge to execute an AWS SSM Automation document for restarting an application, it is essential to consider the monitoring strategy used. While StatsD helps track performance metrics, it is not the most effective means of ensuring that the application is immediately restarted after unexpected downtimes. StatsD is not primarily designed to monitor application states, so relying solely on this tool can compromise the effectiveness of the automation.

The option that says: Configure the CloudWatch agent to monitor the application’s process using procstat monitoring. Set up a CloudWatch alarm to trigger an AWS Systems Manager Automation document for immediate EC2 instance restart upon application failure is incorrect. The use of procstat monitoring helps monitor an application’s processes. However, it is essential to note that the recommended action in case of an application failure is to trigger an SSM Automation document for an immediate EC2 instance restart. This option could be disruptive, as restarting the entire instance could unnecessarily affect other processes or applications running on the same instance. Restarting only the application instead of the whole server is more efficient and less intrusive unless the server itself is compromised.

questions.question:
A company wants to investigate the high data transfer charges of its cloud infrastructure. The CloudOps Engineer has been tasked to monitor and manage expenses related to data transfers between different AWS Regions.

To ensure cost control, the Engineer must set up a system that sends notifications to a designated corporate email distribution list when the data transfer costs approach 70% of the predetermined threshold.

How can the CloudOps Engineer fulfill these demands?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service sends email notifications when inter-region data transfer costs approach a defined budget threshold?
choices: Generate an AWS Cost and Usage Report and analyze the report using Amazon Athena. Configure Amazon SNS to send email notifications when data transfer expenses approach 70% of the predefined threshold. Subscribe to the designated email distribution list for the topic.

Utilize AWS Budgets to set up a cost budget exclusively for data transfer expenses. Implement an alert when costs approach 70% of the budgeted amount. Configure the budget to notify the designated email distribution list when costs exceed 70% of the threshold.

Deploy a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) flow log. Set up a CloudWatch subscription filter linked to an AWS Lambda function for data transfer analysis between AWS Regions. Configure the Lambda function to notify the designated email distribution list when data transfer costs approach 70% of the predefined threshold.

Create a billing alarm in AWS Cost Explorer to send an alert when data transfer costs exceed 70% of the threshold. Connect the billing alarm to an Amazon SNS topic and have the designated email distribution list be subscribed to this topic
correctAnswers: Utilize AWS Budgets to set up a cost budget exclusively for data transfer expenses. Implement an alert when costs approach 70% of the budgeted amount. Configure the budget to notify the designated email distribution list when costs exceed 70% of the threshold.
questions.explanation:
AWS Budgets is an excellent tool that enables users to set and track their spending limits. One of the most exciting features of AWS Budget is that users can set up custom alerts and notifications to help them stay on top of their spending and usage. For example, users can utilize AWS Budgets to create a cost budget exclusively for data transfer expenses. This allows them to monitor their data transfer spending and ensure they stay within their budget.

You can create budgets to track and take action on your costs and usage. You can also create budgets to track your aggregate Reserved Instance (RI) and Savings Plans utilization and coverage. By default, single accounts, management account, and member accounts in an organization can create budgets.

When you create a budget, AWS Budgets provides a Cost Explorer graph to help you see your incurred costs and usage. If you haven’t enabled Cost Explorer yet, this graph is blank, and AWS Budgets will enable Cost Explorer when you create your first budget. You can create your budget without enabling Cost Explorer. It can take up to 24 hours for this graph to appear after you or AWS Budgets enable Cost Explorer.

Another helpful feature of AWS Budget is the ability to implement alerts when costs approach a certain threshold. For instance, users can configure the budget to notify the designated email distribution list when costs exceed 70% of the budgeted amount. This alert system helps users stay informed about their spending and make necessary adjustments to their AWS usage. Users can use AWS Budgets to manage their AWS usage and spending effectively and efficiently, saving money and avoiding unexpected charges.

When you create a budget that sends notifications to an Amazon Simple Notification Service (Amazon SNS) topic, you need to either have a preexisting Amazon SNS topic or create one. Amazon SNS topics allow you to send notifications over SNS in addition to email. Your budget must have permission to send a notification to your topic.

Hence, the correct answer is: Utilize AWS Budgets to set up a cost budget exclusively for data transfer expenses. Implement an alert when costs approach 70% of the budgeted amount. Configure the budget to notify the designated email distribution list when costs exceed 70% of the threshold.

The option that says: Generate an AWS Cost and Usage Report and analyze the report using Amazon Athena. Configure Amazon SNS to send email notifications when data transfer expenses approach 70% of the predefined threshold. Subscribe to the designated email distribution list for the topic is incorrect. This approach involves manually generating cost and usage reports and analyzing them with Amazon Athena, which introduces unnecessary complexity compared to AWS Budget, which provides more direct solutions for expense monitoring. In addition, there’s no built-in alarm feature in Amazon SNS that you can configure.

The option that says: Create a billing alarm in AWS Cost Explorer to send an alert when data transfer costs exceed 70% of the threshold. Connect the billing alarm to an Amazon SNS topic and have the designated email distribution list be subscribed to this topic is incorrect. The AWS Cost Explorer service only provides an easy-to-use interface that lets you visualize, understand, and manage your AWS costs and usage over time. This is not a suitable service to use in setting up a billing alarm to track the data transfer costs of your AWS account. AWS Cost Explorer just lacks the budget-focused approach that’s needed to manage data transfer expenses effectively. Setting up alarms for cost tracking might involve more configuration and management overhead.

The option that says: Deploy a Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) flow log. Set up a CloudWatch subscription filter linked to an AWS Lambda function for data transfer analysis between AWS Regions. Configure the Lambda function to notify the designated email distribution list when data transfer costs approach 70% of the predefined threshold is incorrect. VPC flow logs are primarily designed for network monitoring and analysis, not for tracking and alerting about cost-related matters. While Lambda Functions can be used for notifications (via calling the SNS API, this approach entails complexity and components which are not directly aligned with managing data transfer expenses within cost thresholds.

questions.question:
A healthcare technology company recently completed a network consolidation initiative, reducing multiple NAT gateways across its Amazon VPC to a single one in order to lower operational overhead. Amazon EC2 instances deployed in private subnets across several Availability Zones began reporting failed outbound internet connections following the change. A CloudOps Engineer must restore connectivity without exposing the instances directly to the public internet.

Which action should the CloudOps Engineer take to restore outbound internet access for the affected instances?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you restore outbound internet access for private subnet instances after consolidating to a single NAT gateway?
choices: Modify each affected private subnet’s route table to direct the 0.0.0.0/0 route toward an internet gateway.
Configure an AWS Transit Gateway attachment and update route tables to route outbound traffic through it.
Update VPC route tables to target an egress-only internet gateway to restore outbound internet connectivity for the private instances.
Modify the route table of each affected private subnet to direct outbound traffic through the existing NAT gateway.
correctAnswers: Modify the route table of each affected private subnet to direct outbound traffic through the existing NAT gateway.
questions.explanation:
When an Amazon VPC is configured with multiple NAT gateways, each private subnet’s route table has a default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to the NAT gateway in its Availability Zone. After the consolidation removed the extra NAT gateways, the route tables in the affected subnets were left with stale entries pointing to resources that no longer exist. The VPC silently drops traffic when a route target is invalid, which explains the loss of outbound connectivity. The fix is to update each affected route table so that its 0.0.0.0/0 route points to the one remaining NAT gateway.

A NAT gateway lets Amazon EC2 instances in private subnets send outbound requests to the internet while blocking unsolicited inbound connections. Route tables control how traffic is directed inside a VPC, and they do not automatically update when a NAT gateway is deleted. A CloudOps Engineer must manually update the route table entries, or use automation tools such as AWS CloudFormation or AWS Systems Manager, to point all affected private subnets to the active NAT gateway.

AWS VPC Console showing a private subnet route table with a 0.0.0.0/0 route in blackhole status, indicating the NAT gateway target no longer exists.

Key points to remember about NAT gateways and route tables:

– A NAT gateway is scoped to a single Availability Zone. The recommended architecture for high availability is one NAT gateway per AZ, with each AZ’s private subnets routed through their local NAT gateway.

– Consolidating to one NAT gateway is an acceptable cost-saving measure, but every private subnet route table across all AZs must be updated to target that single NAT gateway ID.

– Deleting a NAT gateway does not trigger any automatic route table updates. Stale routes must be corrected manually or through infrastructure automation using tools such as CloudFormation or Systems Manager.

The other options do not fix a route table misconfiguration and would not restore outbound connectivity in this scenario.

Hence, the correct answer is: Modify the route table of each affected private subnet to direct outbound traffic through the existing NAT gateway.

The option that says: Update VPC route tables to target an egress-only internet gateway to restore outbound internet connectivity for the private instances is incorrect. An egress-only internet gateway only supports IPv6 traffic. Since EC2 instances in private subnets typically use IPv4 by default, this option would have no effect on the connectivity issue caused by the NAT gateway consolidation.

The option that says: Modify each affected private subnet’s route table to direct the 0.0.0.0/0 route toward an internet gateway is incorrect. An internet gateway requires instances to have public IP addresses to function. EC2 instances in private subnets do not have public IPs, so routing their traffic to an internet gateway would not restore connectivity and would contradict the requirement to keep them private.

The option that says: Configure an AWS Transit Gateway attachment and update route tables to route outbound traffic through it is incorrect. Transit Gateway is primarily built for connecting multiple VPCs and on-premises networks, not for providing outbound internet access within a single VPC. It does not offer NAT functionality and would not resolve the routing issue described in this scenario.

questions.question:
A company has tasked a CloudOps Engineer with creating VPC flow logs. The engineer configured the logs using the default format and set Amazon CloudWatch Logs as the destination. The solution has been operating for several weeks, providing valuable insights into network traffic within the Virtual Private Cloud (VPC).

The networking team has introduced new troubleshooting requirements, requesting more detailed information about TCP flags in the network traffic. To meet this requirement, the engineer must include the tcp-flag fields in the flow logs to fulfill this request.

Which solution should the CloudOps Engineer implement to fulfill this requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you add tcp-flags to an existing VPC flow log that uses the default format?
choices: From the CloudWatch Logs log group, set up a new metric filter. Specify the tcp-flags field as the filter pattern.
Update the existing flow log. Select the Custom log record format. Choose fields including tcp-flags. Review and save the configuration.
Set up a new flow log. Select the Custom log record format. Choose fields including tcp-flags. Remove the old flow log.
From the CloudWatch Logs log group console, click view in the Logs Insights. Run a query to see logs with the tcp-flags field.
correctAnswers: Set up a new flow log. Select the Custom log record format. Choose fields including tcp-flags. Remove the old flow log.
questions.explanation:
VPC Flow Logs is a feature that enables you to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in your VPC. Flow log data can be published to the following locations: Amazon CloudWatch Logs, Amazon S3, or Amazon Data Firehose. After you create a flow log, you can retrieve and view the flow log records in the log group, bucket, or delivery stream that you configured.

You can create flow logs for your VPCs, subnets, or network interfaces. When you create a flow log, you must specify a destination for the flow log. Upon creating a flow log for a subnet or VPC, each network interface in that subnet or VPC is monitored.

When creating flow logs you must select which log record format you are going to use. There are two types of log record format:

Default format – With the default format, the flow log records include the version 2 fields. You cannot customize or change the default format. To capture additional fields or a different subset of fields, specify a custom format instead.

Custom format – With a custom format, you specify which fields are included in the flow log records and in which order. This enables you to create flow logs that are specific to your needs and to omit fields that are not relevant. Using a custom format can reduce the need for separate processes to extract specific information from the published flow logs. You can specify any number of the available flow log fields, but you must specify at least one.

VPC Flow Logs

The tcp-flags field is not included in the default format, you must use a custom format to include the tcp-flags field in the flow log.

After you create a flow log, you cannot change its configuration or the flow log record format. For example, you can’t associate a different IAM role with the flow log, or add or remove fields in the flow log record. Instead, you can delete the flow log and create a new one with the required configuration.

When you need to create a change in your existing flow logs. You must remove it and create a new flow log with the required configuration.

Hence, the correct answer is: Set up a new flow log. Select the Custom log record format. Choose fields including tcp-flags. Remove the old flow log.

The option that says: From the CloudWatch Logs log group, set up a new metric filter. Specify the tcp-flags field as the filter pattern is incorrect. While creating a metric filter will help in monitoring the logs, it still remains inadequate because the metric filter will not be able to filter data for the tcp-flags field as the flow log is not configured to include the tcp-flags field in the first place. Metric filters typically allow you to search and filter log data coming into CloudWatch Logs, extract metric observations from the filtered log data, and transform the data points into a CloudWatch Logs metric. However, there are no logs regarding the tcp-flags field in the existing log group. Hence, creating a metric filter will not resolve the issue. As explained above, you must create a new flow log that includes the tcp-flags.

The option that says: From the CloudWatch Logs log group console, click view in the Logs Insights. Run a query to see logs with the tcp-flags field is incorrect because even though viewing the logs in Log Insights will allow you to run a query and easily search for the data in the existing log groups, it will not return any results regarding the tcp-flags as the existing flow log is not configured to include tcp-flags field.

The option that says: Update the existing flow log. Select the Custom log record format. Choose fields including tcp-flags. Review and save the configuration is incorrect because you cannot just update the configuration of an existing flow log. You must create a new flow log and remove the old one instead.

questions.question:
An IT Manager is managing an Amazon S3 website and needs to ensure that it is accessible only through an Amazon CloudFront distribution. The environment uses Amazon Route 53 with a multivalue answer routing policy to distribute traffic. The manager wants to prevent visitors from bypassing CloudFront and accessing the S3 website directly via the bucket URL.

Which action would satisfy the requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you restrict access to an S3 website so it can only be reached through CloudFront?
choices: Enable the IgnorePublicAcls setting on the S3 bucket to block public access granted by ACLs.
Use Lambda@Edge to run custom logic closer to users
Create an origin access control (OAC) to restrict S3 bucket access to CloudFront.
Enable S3 Access Points to manage access.
correctAnswers: Create an origin access control (OAC) to restrict S3 bucket access to CloudFront.
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudFront is a web service designed to accelerate the delivery of static and dynamic web content, such as.html,.css,.js, and image files, to users. It achieves this by using a global network of data centers known as edge locations. Users who request content served via CloudFront are directed to the edge location with the lowest latency, ensuring optimal performance.

Origin Access Control (OAC) is a feature introduced by Amazon CloudFront to enhance the security of Amazon S3 origins. It allows you to restrict access to your S3 bucket so that it is only accessible through your CloudFront distribution. OAC uses short-term credentials and frequent credential rotations.

Origin Access Control

To implement this, you need to:

Create an Origin Access Control (OAC) for CloudFront: Update the S3 bucket policy to allow access only from the OAC. Hence, the correct answer is: Create an origin access control (OAC) to restrict S3 bucket access to CloudFront.

The option that says: Enable S3 Access Points to manage access is incorrect. While S3 Access Points allow for managing access to shared datasets, they do not inherently restrict access to S3 bucket content through CloudFront without additional configuration.

The option that says: Use Lambda@Edge to run custom logic closer to users is incorrect because this feature is primarily used for running code closer to the user for the purposes of improving performance.

The option that says: Enable the IgnorePublicAcls setting on the S3 bucket to block public access granted by ACLs is incorrect because this setting only blocks public ACLs from granting access. It does not restrict direct access to the bucket URL, so visitors could still bypass CloudFront if other permissions allow it.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer is working for a financial institution that heavily relies on AWS CloudFormation to manage its complex cloud infrastructure. As part of the ongoing efforts to improve operational efficiency, the Engineer conducts a thorough review of the company’s CloudFormation practices in deploying their applications.

A recurring issue was found during the investigation where exactly the same components are defined in multiple CloudFormation templates in a single AWS account and region. This duplication is not only time-consuming but also increases the risk of configuration inconsistencies and security vulnerabilities. The company needs to create dedicated templates with its own set of parameters and conditions for the common components to optimize its deployment process.

What is the most operationally efficient way to satisfy the above requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which CloudFormation feature eliminates duplicate resource definitions by reusing common components across templates?
choices: Use AWS CloudFormation nested stacks.
Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets.
Use AWS CloudFormation macros.
Use AWS CloudFormation registry.
correctAnswers: Use AWS CloudFormation nested stacks.
questions.explanation:
Nested stacks are stacks created as part of other stacks. You can create a nested stack within another stack by using the AWS::CloudFormation::Stack resource.

As your infrastructure grows, common patterns can emerge in which you declare the same components in multiple templates. You can separate out these common components and create dedicated templates for them. Then use the resource in your template to reference other templates, creating nested stacks.

For example, assume that you have a load balancer configuration that you use for most of your stacks. Instead of copying and pasting the same configurations into your templates, you can create a dedicated template for the load balancer. Then, you just use the AWS::CloudFormation::Stack resource to reference that template from within other templates.

If the load balancer template is updated, any stack that is referencing it will use the updated load balancer (only after you update the stack). In addition to simplifying updates, this approach lets you use experts to create and maintain components that you might not necessarily be familiar with. All you need to do is reference their templates.

Hence, the correct answer is: Use AWS CloudFormation nested stacks.

The option that says: Use AWS CloudFormation registry is incorrect. The CloudFormation registry feature only allows you to effectively manage public and private extensions, including resources, modules, and hooks that can be utilized in your AWS account. While this is a useful tool for managing custom resources, it is not the best solution for preventing duplicate resource definitions across various templates. Using CloudFormation nested stacks is still the ideal way to separate out the common components and create dedicated templates for them.

The option that says: Use AWS CloudFormation macros is incorrect because these just enable you to perform custom processing on your CloudFormation templates. It allows you to do simple actions like find-and-replace operations to extensive transformations of entire templates; however, it cannot be used in creating dedicated templates with its own set of parameters and conditions for the common components to optimize its deployment process.

The option that says: Use AWS CloudFormation StackSets is incorrect because a stack set simply enables you to create, update, or delete stacks across multiple accounts and AWS Regions with a single operation. It is not the appropriate solution for preventing the same components from being defined in multiple CloudFormation templates.

questions.question:
A company has established a NAT gateway within a public subnet of its Virtual Private Cloud. The VPC also hosts a private subnet used by Amazon EC2 instances. These instances utilize the NAT gateway for internet connectivity to fetch necessary software updates and patches. The company has set up VPC flow logs for the NAT gateway’s Elastic Network Interface (ENI), and the data is sent to Amazon CloudWatch Logs for monitoring. The CloudOps Engineer must determine the three most frequently accessed internet destinations by the EC2 instance in the private subnet for download purposes.

What is the MOST operationally efficient way that the CloudOps Engineer can implement to satisfy the requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which tool best identifies the top internet destinations accessed by private EC2 instances via a NAT gateway using VPC flow logs?
choices: Utilize CloudWatch Logs Insights queries to reveal the top three external destinations accessed.
Use Amazon CloudFront real-time logs to identify the top three internet destinations.
Utilize Amazon Inspector to assess network configurations and identify security vulnerabilities related to internet communications.
Leverage AWS CloudTrail Insights events to determine the three most frequently accessed internet endpoints.
correctAnswers: Utilize CloudWatch Logs Insights queries to reveal the top three external destinations accessed.
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights is a powerful interactive log analysis service in AWS that allows users to explore, analyze, and visualize their logs in Amazon CloudWatch. With CloudWatch Logs Insights, you can run queries to filter, parse, and summarize logging data, making it possible to pinpoint specific external destinations that instances are communicating with.

CloudWatch Logs Insights

CloudWatch Logs Insights is specifically designed for log analysis, including examining VPC Flow Logs, to extract insights such as external communication destinations. Its query language and visualization capabilities make it an ideal tool for this task.

Hence, the correct answer is: Utilize CloudWatch Logs Insights queries to reveal the top three external destinations accessed.

The option that says: Leverage AWS CloudTrail Insights events to determine the three most frequently accessed internet endpoints is incorrect because CloudTrail is typically used for tracking API calls and user activity within AWS, rather than analyzing network traffic to external destinations. CloudTrail is invaluable for audit trails and governance, but does not provide the necessary data for this specific use case.

The option that says: Utilize Amazon Inspector to assess network configurations and identify security vulnerabilities related to internet communications is incorrect. Amazon Inspector is a security assessment service primarily designed to help improve the security and compliance of applications on AWS. While it assesses applications for vulnerabilities and deviations from best practices, it does not analyze network traffic data to identify internet destinations.

The option that says: Use Amazon CloudFront real-time logs to identify the top three internet destinations is incorrect because Amazon CloudFront logs are related to monitoring and analyzing web distributions, which are unrelated to the outbound internet connections made by EC2 instances using a NAT gateway.

questions.question:
A digital media company relies on Amazon CloudFront to deliver static content, including images, to its audience. The marketing department has newly added 175 new static assets to the website, but some of them do not appear correctly for users. Upon investigation, the Cloud Engineer discovers that the default Time-to-Live (TTL) for the CloudFront cache is set to 5 days. The users are experiencing delays in seeing the updated content.

What is the MOST efficient way of refreshing the cache and ensuring the new assets are displayed immediately?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is the fastest way to force CloudFront to serve newly updated static assets before the TTL expires?
choices: Set up a new CloudFront distribution with the same origin, configure the default TTL to 1 minute (60 seconds), and update the Amazon Route 53 DNS records to the new distribution.
Modify the existing CloudFront distribution to change the default TTL to 1 minute (60 seconds) and add the flag to invalidate objects in the specified path during the configuration update.
Configure Lambda@Edge to invalidate the cache whenever the images are updated.
Perform a CloudFront invalidation to clear the outdated images from the cache instantly.
correctAnswers: Perform a CloudFront invalidation to clear the outdated images from the cache instantly.
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudFront is a content delivery network (CDN) service that reliably provides data, videos, apps, and APIs to customers worldwide with low latency and fast transfer speeds. CloudFront invalidation allows you to remove specific objects from the cache before they expire, ensuring that users receive the most current content. Issuing an invalidation request is an effective way to refresh the cache without waiting for the TTL to expire naturally.

By initiating a CloudFront invalidation request, the administrator can promptly remove the outdated images from the cache, ensuring that the new images are displayed to users. This method is efficient because it targets specific objects and avoids the complexity of creating a new distribution or changing image locations. Invalidation requests are straightforward to implement and provide immediate results, making them the optimal choice for refreshing specific cached content quickly.

Hence, the correct answer is: Perform a CloudFront invalidation to clear the outdated images from the cache instantly.

The option that says: Set up a new CloudFront distribution with the same origin, configure the default TTL to 1 minute (60 seconds), and update the Amazon Route 53 DNS records to the new distribution is incorrect because creating a new distribution and updating DNS records is a time-consuming process that involves additional complexity and potential downtime. This approach also introduces the risk of DNS propagation delays, which can further delay the availability of new images to end users.

The option that says: Configure Lambda@Edge to invalidate the cache whenever the images are updated is incorrect because configuring Lambda@Edge for this purpose only adds unnecessary complexity and operational overhead. Lambda@Edge is designed for executing logic closer to the end users, and using it for cache invalidation requires additional configuration and maintenance. Moreover, it can incur higher costs due to the execution of functions at the edge locations, making it less cost-effective for simple cache invalidation tasks.

The option that says: Modify the existing CloudFront distribution to change the default TTL to 1 minute (60 seconds) and add the flag to invalidate objects in the specified path during the configuration update is incorrect because changing the TTL simply affects the caching strategy for all objects within the specified path, potentially leading to frequent cache invalidations and higher origin request rates. This approach can increase the load on the origin server and reduce the overall cache hit ratio, negatively impacting the performance and cost-efficiency of the CloudFront distribution.

questions.question:
A company has multiple AWS accounts under AWS Organizations. Each team has its own account. Each administrator has root user credentials for their respective member account. The company has recently finished DynamoDB cleanup and wants to ensure that no one can deploy DynamoDB resources anywhere. The CloudOps Engineer is tasked to prevent all teams from accessing the Amazon DynamoDB service without affecting their access to other AWS services. This should apply to all users, including the team Administrators.

Which solution will satisfy the requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you block DynamoDB access for all users across all AWS Organization accounts, including root users?
choices: In the management account, detach the default SCP. Configure a service control policy (SCP). Deny all DynamoDB actions in the policy. Attach the replacement SCP to the root of the organization.
In each member account, create an IAM Permission boundary that allows actions for all services except for Amazon DynamoDB. Attach the permission boundary to the root user and all IAM users
In the management account, configure a service control policy (SCP). Deny all DynamoDB actions in the policy. Attach the SCP to the root of the organization.
In each member account, configure an IAM policy to deny AmazonDynamoDBFullAccess. Attach the policy to the root user and all IAM users.
correctAnswers: In the management account, configure a service control policy (SCP). Deny all DynamoDB actions in the policy. Attach the SCP to the root of the organization.
questions.explanation:
Service control policies (SCPs) are a type of organization policy that you can use to manage permissions in your organization. SCPs offer central control over the maximum available permissions for all accounts in your organization. SCPs help you to ensure your accounts stay within your organization’s access control guidelines.

An SCP defines a guardrail or sets limits, on the actions that the account’s administrator can delegate to the IAM users and roles in the affected accounts. The administrator must still attach identity-based or resource-based policies to IAM users or roles, or to the resources in your accounts to actually grant permissions. The effective permissions are the logical intersection between what is allowed by the SCP and what is allowed by the IAM and resource-based policies.

The default configuration for working with SCPs is to use a “block list” strategy where all actions are implicitly allowed except for those actions you want to block by creating statements that deny access. With deny statements, you can specify resources and conditions for the statement and use the NotAction element. For allow statements, you can specify services and actions only.

When you sign in to your organization’s management account, you can attach a service control policy (SCP) that you previously created. You can attach an SCP to the organization root, to an organizational unit (OU), or directly to an account.

To deny all users, including root users to all member accounts, the SysOps administrator can create a SCP with deny statements and attach it to the root organization.

Hence, the correct answer is: In the management account, configure a service control policy (SCP). Deny all DynamoDB actions in the policy. Attach the SCP to the root of the organization.

The option that says: In each member account, configure an IAM policy to deny AmazonDynamoDBFullAccess. Attach the policy to the root user and all IAM users is incorrect. While you can control which IAM entity an IAM policy can be attached to or detached from, it’s not possible to deny an IAM policy as a whole. Therefore, this solution is not possible. As an alternative, you can create a custom policy that denies all DynamoDB actions to all resources by using wildcard (dynamodb:*Resource:*) and attach it to all IAM users. However, this only partially meets the requirement, as you cannot use IAM policies to explicitly deny a root user’s access to resources.

The option that says: In each member account, create an IAM Permission boundary that allows actions for all services except for Amazon DynamoDB. Attach the permission boundary to the root user and all IAM users are incorrect. While Permission Boundaries can implicitly deny permissions by specifically excluding unwanted actions, this approach still involves high operational overhead because it requires individual setup for each IAM user across all accounts. Moreover, permission boundaries are only applicable to IAM users or IAM roles, not root users.

The option that says: In the management account, detach the default SCP. Configure a service control policy (SCP). Deny all DynamoDB actions in the policy. Attach the replacement SCP to the root of the organization is incorrect. This solution cannot be used for this use case as it is impossible to detach the default SCP. Every root, OU, and account must have at least one SCP attached. If you want to replace the default policy, you must attach the replacement SCP before you can remove the default SCP. However, exercising caution is advised when considering the replacement of the default SCP attached to the root. The default SCP serves as a baseline policy that provides a certain level of restrictions on member accounts within the organization.
